The topic of self-driving cars is highly debated especially in light of recent event with the death of an Arizona woman at the hands of a self-driving Uber. Those for self-driving cars argue that they reduce the rate of accidents as a result of distracted drivers, improve traffic flow, are cost saving in terms of time and monetary value from insurance, and could allow greater access to transportation for the disabled and elderly. Those against self-driving cars argue the system does not eliminate accidents, that there is a learning curve with the technology, that it would take away profit from other sectors of the economy, and that there are instances in which the technology is not equipped to make a decision. While both sides make valid points, the benefits of adopting a system of self-driving cars rises to the forefront. 

Many people are quick to point out the flaws in self-driving cars but it is still in its infancy and has the ability to develop and improve with time whereas we have not seen that in human motorists over the years. Certainly we may expect the number of accidents by self-driving cars to rise as it is more widely adopted but this number palls in comparison to the number of accidents caused by drivers. Tragedies such as those in Arizona should not happen but unfortunately with nearly 1.3 million people dying in motor vehicle accidents a year and 81 percent of accidents being the result of human error it appears the main culprit is us. As humans we already rely on computers and technology for many daily tasks and self-driving cars are an extension of the innovative mindset our tech fields have embraced. 

Those against also argue that investing in self-driving cars may take away money from other industries and sectors of the economy. Again while self-driving cars may impact the profits of other transportation system, overall the profits from developing and implementing this technology will outweigh the losses. There is a reason many people invest in tech companies as the have become more profitable in the 21st century. Especially with the start-up of new technology, as they industry grows this will demand that more and more jobs be created to replace the ones lost. Also, savings in terms of reduced accidents are astronomical. The U.S. Department of Transportation has assigned a value of 9.2 million for each human life, with savings coming from venues such as healthcare and insurance. There is also the argument that self-driving cars may also save you time as they free up your transportation time to attend to other daily tasks when you do not have to focus on the driving. With more efficient driving the self-driving cars have the potential to decrease driving time and traffic as well.

Though people are still resistant to the idea of self-driving cars it is already in ways being implemented into our driving with features such as autonomous braking, self-parking and sensor detectors. The technology for this field is currently available and calls on our investment to develop and improve. While no system is perfect, one that saves us lives and on costs should be highly considered.

output_text:
The topic of self-driving cars is highly debated especially in light of recent event with the death of an Arizona woman at the hands of a self-driving Uber. Those for self-driving cars argue that they reduce the rate of accidents as a result of distracted drivers, improve traffic flow, are cost saving in terms of time and monetary value from insurance, and could allow greater access to transportation for the disabled and elderly. Those against self-driving cars argue the system does not eliminate accidents, that there is a learning curve with the technology, that it would take away profit from other sectors of the economy, and that there are instances in which the technology is not equipped to make a decision. While both sides make valid points, the benefits of adopting a system of self-driving cars rises to the forefront.

Many people are quick to point out the flaws in self-driving cars but it is still in its infancy and has the ability to develop and improve with time whereas we have not seen that in human motorists over the years. Certainly we may expect the number of accidents by self-driving cars to rise as it is more widely adopted but this number palls in comparison to the number of accidents caused by drivers. Tragedies such as those in Arizona should not happen but unfortunately with nearly 1.3 million people dying in motor vehicle accidents a year and 81 percent of accidents being the result of human error it appears the main culprit is us. As humans we already rely on computers and technology for many daily tasks and self-driving cars are an extension of the innovative mindset our tech fields have embraced.

Beyond the immediate safety concerns, the broader economic and societal advantages of autonomous vehicles present a compelling case for their integration. Critics often voice apprehension about job displacement in sectors like professional driving; however, this perspective overlooks the substantial economic growth and new employment opportunities that such a transformative technology invariably creates. The development, manufacturing, maintenance, and oversight of autonomous fleets will necessitate a vast new workforce, from software engineers and AI trainers to specialized mechanics and regulatory compliance officers. Furthermore, the efficiency gains from self-driving cars—including optimized traffic flow, reduced fuel consumption, and fewer accidents—are projected by organizations like the U.S. Department of Transportation to yield billions in annual savings, not only in terms of human lives valued at $9.2 million each, but also in reduced healthcare costs, insurance premiums, and productivity lost to traffic congestion.

Perhaps one of the most profound benefits of self-driving technology lies in its potential to revolutionize accessibility and enhance the quality of life for millions. For the elderly and individuals with disabilities, autonomous vehicles promise unprecedented levels of independence and mobility, freeing them from reliance on public transportation schedules or the availability of caregivers. This newfound freedom can significantly improve social inclusion, access to healthcare, and participation in community life. While concerns about the technology's ability to navigate complex, unpredictable scenarios are valid, the continuous advancements in artificial intelligence, sensor fusion, and machine learning are rapidly addressing these limitations. The systems are designed to learn from vast datasets and real-world experience, making them increasingly robust and capable of handling nuanced decision-making, often outperforming human reaction times and attention spans in critical situations.

In conclusion, while the path to widespread adoption of self-driving cars is fraught with challenges and requires rigorous testing and ethical consideration, the overarching benefits they offer are simply too significant to dismiss. From drastically improving road safety by systematically eliminating human error, to catalyzing new economic sectors and generating efficiencies across society, and profoundly enhancing personal mobility for those currently underserved, autonomous vehicles represent a monumental leap forward. Embracing this technology is not merely a matter of convenience; it is an investment in a future where transportation is safer, more equitable, more efficient, and ultimately, more aligned with humanity's ongoing pursuit of innovation and progress.

Self-driving car is a new technology and receives a lot of debates recently. On one hand, self-driving cars offer positive impacts, e.g. safer commuting, cost savings, and give opportunities to many people who can't travel by themselves at this moment. On the other hand, people also raise concerns about the security, safety, and economy of the technology.
In my opinion, I support bringing self-driving cars into reality. The technology can bring many benefits to humanity. First, we, as human in modern societies, will save a lot of money thanks to the technology. Because when a lot of self-driving cars are on the street and they can communicate with each other, they can reduce the problem with congestion or unknown traffic conditions. Second, self-driving cars are supposed to be more safety because it can eliminate mistakes made by a human. The most common mistake is driving under the influence (of alcohol, drugs, or tireness). Moreover, drivers tend to have false confidence as they have more driving hours. This false confidence will increase the chances that an accident will happen. Last but not least, self-driving cars put an equity for people having difficulties in traveling, such as old people or disabilities. While public transportations are available in all cities, in many cases, they can only take a person to a pre-designated location (stop) rather than the real destination. Public transportations are operated with a fixed schedule which does not perfectly match everyone's situations. 
The cons about self-driving cars could be categorized into two groups: not-ready and not-true. The not-ready group contains arguments about new challenges that the technology will bring in when it is deployed in mass. For example, when an accident is made by a self-driving car, who will hold the responsibility? Or security issues such as how can drivers protect their personal data and what will happen if the car is hijacked by a hi-tech criminal? Interestingly, what would the car-makers or software designers choose in moral-sensitive situations? For example, given an unavoidable accident will happen, which will either kill an old pedestrian crossing the street or a baby in a stroller on the pavement, which choice should be made by the self-driving car? These issues must be addressed or disclosed before deploying self-driving cars in the mass. The not-true group consists of false assumptions about the self-driving technology. For example, there are concerns about drivers are no longer equipped with the skills to operate cars. In fact, we are living well in a society without knowing much about our daily stuffs. I don't know anything about fixing a car, but I can always call AAA or my friends whenever my car breaking down. I use the washing machine at least twice a week without knowing how to make such a machine. Another false opinion is the difference between states regarding turning right on red or cars cannot interpret police officers' signals when directing traffics. In fact, the car can update its algorithm according to the state it is traveling simply based on GPS signal. Moreover, when all cars on the streets are self-driving cars, the police officer does not need to go down the street and directs the traffic but just sends information to the car through a communication channel, e.g. 3G or wifi. Last but not least, some people even afraid that auto manufacturers might sell fewer cars. This is not true as a self-driving car is still a car which needs a manufacturer. In fact, we have seen more and more car manufacturers join the self-driving race nowadays, such as Volvo, BMW, Toyota, Honda, and many more.
In conclusion, I think self-driving car is a revolution that will have more benefit to our society. However, there are still some concerns needed to be addressed in order to make this technology available to everyone.

output_text:
Self-driving cars represent a burgeoning technology that has recently sparked considerable public debate. Proponents highlight their potential for positive impacts, such as enhanced commuting safety, significant cost savings, and increased accessibility for individuals currently unable to travel independently. Conversely, critics voice concerns regarding the technology's security vulnerabilities, overall safety protocols, and economic implications. This essay advocates for the widespread adoption of self-driving cars, arguing that their benefits to humanity far outweigh the perceived drawbacks.

Firstly, modern societies stand to realize substantial financial savings and efficiency gains through autonomous vehicles. When a critical mass of self-driving vehicles operates and communicates synergistically on our roads, they can drastically mitigate traffic congestion and adapt instantly to unforeseen road conditions. This intelligent coordination leads to more efficient travel, reduced fuel consumption, and ultimately, lower transportation costs for individuals and economies alike.

Secondly, self-driving cars promise enhanced safety by largely eliminating human error, which is currently a primary cause of accidents. Foremost among these human errors are impaired driving (due to alcohol, drugs, or fatigue) and distracted driving. Furthermore, experienced drivers sometimes develop a false sense of confidence, which can paradoxically increase the likelihood of accidents. Autonomous systems, free from these human frailties, are designed to adhere strictly to traffic laws and react with consistent precision, thereby significantly reducing accident rates.

Finally, autonomous vehicles offer unprecedented equity in transportation for individuals with mobility challenges, such as the elderly or those with disabilities. While public transportation is available in many urban areas, it often only serves pre-designated stops and operates on fixed schedules, failing to provide true door-to-door service or flexible timing tailored to individual needs. Self-driving cars can bridge this gap, offering personalized, on-demand mobility that empowers a wider segment of the population to travel independently and access opportunities previously out of reach.

While the prospect of autonomous vehicles is exciting, it is important to address the concerns raised by skeptics. These can broadly be categorized into legitimate "not-ready" issues that require further development and "not-true" misconceptions based on flawed assumptions. The "not-ready" category encompasses valid concerns about the novel challenges that will arise with mass deployment. For instance, questions of liability in autonomous vehicle accidents remain complex and require clear legal frameworks. Furthermore, robust cybersecurity measures are essential to protect personal data and prevent malicious hacking or hijacking. Ethical dilemmas, such as programming a vehicle to make impossible moral choices in unavoidable accident scenarios (e.g., choosing between two innocent lives), also demand careful consideration and public discourse. These complex issues necessitate comprehensive solutions and transparent disclosure before widespread deployment can proceed responsibly.

Conversely, the "not-true" category addresses common misconceptions about self-driving technology. One such concern is the belief that drivers will lose the essential skills to operate a vehicle manually. However, this overlooks how modern society functions; individuals routinely utilize complex technologies without needing to understand their intricate mechanics. For example, most people use washing machines daily without knowing how to repair them, or rely on roadside assistance for car breakdowns without possessing mechanical expertise. The ability to drive manually will likely become a specialized skill, not a universal necessity. Another misconception relates to the ability of autonomous vehicles to navigate varied traffic laws (e.g., turning right on red) or interpret human signals from police officers. In reality, these vehicles can be programmed to update their algorithms based on GPS location to comply with local regulations. Furthermore, in a fully autonomous ecosystem, traffic management could evolve to digital communication channels (e.g., 5G, Wi-Fi), rendering manual direction by officers largely obsolete. Finally, the fear that autonomous driving will reduce car sales is unfounded. A self-driving car is still a vehicle requiring manufacture and purchase. Indeed, the increasing number of major automakers, including Volvo, BMW, and Toyota, investing heavily in autonomous technology demonstrates a clear belief in its market longevity and profitability.

In conclusion, self-driving cars represent a transformative technological revolution poised to deliver substantial societal benefits. While legitimate concerns regarding safety, ethics, and legal frameworks must be meticulously addressed, these challenges are surmountable. With continued innovation and thoughtful regulation, autonomous vehicles hold the promise of a safer, more accessible, and more efficient future for transportation.

draft1:
Self-driving cars, though only recently becoming a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development and have thoroughly captured the public eye. The debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole is raging onward with impassioned argument on both sides. In this essay I will aim to provide convincing evidence that though it is not perfect, the prospect of driver-less roads would mark a definitive improvement over our current conditions. 

To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. Although it is not yet certain that self-driving cars in their current condition would cause that number to go down, the lack of human fallibility makes the prospect seem likely. Furthermore, whereas human driver tend to fall into a lull of false confidence when they have engaged in a task for an extended period of time, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. There is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. 

Furthermore, self-driving cars could give society a method of transportation which is much less restrictive. Currently, driving privileges are limited to those capable of operating the machinery, passing their test, and -for the most part - affording to own their own vehicle. If a child needs to be transported to any sort of extracurricular event they are bound by their parents' schedules and mobilities. If a senior citizen who has been deemed unsafe to drive decides to go shopping they must wait for long periods to use public transportation with conditions that could be extremely painful or uncomfortable with those who experience chronic pain from arthritis or similar conditions. Furthermore, many who cannot afford their own car are forced to commute long distances to their jobs, health-care, and programs, forcing a de facto restriction on who may access what. Although it is likely that one who cannot afford a regular car could not afford their own self-driving car, there is nothing to say that they must have their own. The ability to have a car drive back to the place it left from could allow a much more intricate form of car sharing; additionally, the current restrictions on insured drivers that hassle any type of shared vehicle would likely be thoroughly relaxed or missing entirely for a driver-less car. Even beyond the prospect of shared cars, self-driving vehicles could revolutionize public transportation, making a wider range of pick up locations and drop off locations feasible. If large buses or a huge fleet of public cars were to have flexible routes that could give people who do not have the means to own their own car a transportational freedom akin to it at a fraction of the price that services like Uber and Lift offer today. 

A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine much of their success and failure. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it does not seem to be a stretch to claim that this could bring us dramatic improvements.

Self-driving cars, though only recently having become a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development, managing to thoroughly capture the public eye. A debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole has been raging onward with impassioned arguments on both sides. In this essay I aim to provide convincing evidence that, though they have potential to cause societal harm, given the distressing array of negative societal impacts associated with human-driven cars, the apparent feasibility for self-driving cars to ameliorate these issues makes it only rational to welcome their implementation. 
	To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these, it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. 
	While no one would rightly argue that our current system is ideal, skepticism toward self-driving cars at this stage is more than fair. The development of the vehicles is very much still in its developmental infancy. Coupling that fact with the reality of a mixed automotive environment - with both computerized and human driven cars - one might very well expect implementation of self-driving cars to cause an increase in accidents. The key question then becomes: "would a self-driving car actually be any safer than a human-driven one?". While there is currently insufficient information to give a definite answer, I think there are good reasons to put more faith in the self-driving cars. Whereas human drivers tend to fall into a lull of false confidence after driving for many years, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. Further, there is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. In the absence of such risks, a computerized car might very well behave safer even without the ability to intuitively grasp tendencies and perceptions of neighboring human drivers. 
	Others however, are more concerned about the societal impact of these cars than their safety specs. Even if the change means less lives lost on the road, it still may be an unwise one if it leads to the ruin of lives off the road. Given the number of transportation jobs, a computerized revolution could displace the occupations of many currently employed drivers. Furthermore, given the massive economic investment needed to own such a car, might this lead to a widening of already egregious inequality gaps in the country? 
	The transportation industry represents a real threat if we have no place for the newly unemployed to turn. This however, is not a problem unique to self-driving cars but rather, is a pervasive and imminent threat encroaching on us from all directions given the rapid advance of technological competency. If we cannot, as a society solve this more generally, the threats will be realized even in the absence of the driver-less car revolution; if we do find a way to support the displaced workers of other sectors I remain hopeful that such approaches can be implemented to the transportation-sector as well. 
	As for societal inequality, self-driving vehicles, if implemented properly, could actually be a great boon to economically struggling classes. To begin, the previously considered transportation industry automation, though bad for workers, could be fantastic for commodity prices. If the cost of all daily goods dropped, many would be the better for it. Further, current systems of public transportation are woefully insufficient to serve their desired societal role. When jobs, hospitals, and enrichment events are inconvenient to access, attendance from under serviced individuals tends to drop. This creates a de-facto restriction on who may access what. With an automated public transportation system, it could be much easier to introduce flexible pick-up locations, drop-off spots, and schedules. With a more functional and convenient public transportation system, we could relieve both the economic burden of buying a vehicle as well as the logistical burden of navigating tricky and time-consuming bus routes. 
	A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine whether those differences were successes or failures. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it is reasonable to maintain optimism about the ability of self-driving cars to bring about dramatic improvements to our lives.

The economic benefits stemming from increased productivity, reduced fuel consumption, and lower insurance costs due to fewer accidents would inject significant capital back into the economy, fostering growth and innovation.

Of course, the transition to a fully autonomous transportation system is not without its challenges and legitimate concerns. Critics often point to the potential for job displacement among professional drivers, the initial high cost of the technology, and complex ethical dilemmas that self-driving cars might encounter in unavoidable accident scenarios. For instance, questions arise about how an autonomous vehicle should prioritize harm in a no-win situation. While these are serious considerations, they are not insurmountable obstacles to progress. Job displacement, while a valid concern, often accompanies significant technological shifts; it necessitates societal adaptation through retraining programs and the emergence of new industries in manufacturing, maintenance, and software development for autonomous systems. Regarding ethical dilemmas, human drivers, too, make split-second decisions with imperfect information and often inconsistent outcomes. Autonomous systems, conversely, can be programmed with transparent, consistent ethical frameworks, continuously refined through data and analysis, aiming to minimize overall harm in ways that human intuition cannot reliably achieve. The initial cost, like all new technologies, is expected to decrease with mass production and widespread adoption, eventually making autonomous transport more accessible and affordable than traditional car ownership.

A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine much of their success and failure. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it does not seem to be a stretch to claim that this could bring us dramatic improvements. Despite the valid concerns and the complex challenges of implementation, the overwhelming potential for enhanced safety, expanded accessibility, and optimized societal efficiency positions self-driving cars as a definitive and beneficial evolution in human transportation, promising a future of safer, more inclusive, and more productive roads for everyone.

The primary benefit is simply the amount of lives saved due to the elimination of intoxicated, distracted, or otherwise dangerous drivers. Computers provide a precise, safe alternative to the potential for human error that occurs each time a driver gets behind the wheel. In addition, self-driving cars can save money on insurance costs and outdated infrastructure, decrease traffic, and are actually better for the environment.  However, some limitations to self-driving cars exist. These include a difficult transition to using these vehicles, cost, the failure of technology, and the potential for job loss and decline of certain industries.
	Frequently, we hear arguments for or against something with the justification that it benefits or does not benefit society.  In order to make an argument for either one of these, it is imperative that we consider the following questions: who is society, what is good for society, what does society need, and at what expense to the individual do we support the public good?
	In this case, the answer to these questions is easy. Anything that has the potential to save human lives is both good for the individual and good for society. Despite the limitations in technology, an initial cost, and a likely challenging transition, self-driving cars are the future, and they will save lives. No other benefit matters. No other limitation should stop us.  
America has a serious, dangerous driving problem. Every year, 33,000 people die in automobile accidents, and most of these deaths should have been prevented because the drivers were distracted, drunk, tired, or simply should not have been driving.  
If that statistic is not sobering enough, consider this. Vehicular accidents are also the number one cause of death for teenagers, again, the majority of which are preventable.  We know from cognitive scientists and psychologists that teenagers are more likely to engage in risky behavior—like dangerous, drunk, or otherwise impaired driving—and, while getting a driver’s license has been a rite-of-passage for a century in America, it’s time to stop the senseless destruction car accidents cause.
Interestingly enough, the Department of Transportation calculated that each person is worth $9.2 million. Like so many things, the government got this one wrong as well. A human life is priceless. Nothing can bring back a loved one who has been killed.
If you have reservations about self-driving cars due of the potential for technological failure, I share them with you. We have miles to go before it can be implemented on any kind of large scale. We have many ethical, legal, and policy considerations and implications to work through. However, we have the opportunity to save countless lives. We have the opportunity to save countless others from debilitating injury. We have the obligation to use technology the way it’s always been used—to benefit our society and improve the lives of all Americans.

When Back to the Future I and II were released in the 80's, many people were captivated by the technology showed throughout the films. Even though technology has greatly advanced within the last decade, it still has not reached the status of flying cars and floating hover boards. As we inch closer to 2020, the idea of having such technology does not seem impossible anymore. Olivier Garret stated in a Forbes article that he believed 10 million self-driving cars will be on the road by 2020, and one out of four cars will be self-driving by 2030. Although Back to the Future II predicted cars flying in 2015, the estimate of 2020 for self-driving cars is not that far off. 

Within the past year, companies like Ford, Honda, BMW and Tesla are few among the many automakers that are pushing for self-driving technology. This makes complete sense as technology is on an increasing incline forwards and will continue to do so as long as there is a need for it. Since the idea of self-driving cars is new and there has not been a ton of research on it, it is important to understand the advantages and disadvantages before getting into an autonomous vehicle. 

Listed here are the highlights of a self-driving car: human error is reduced due to the machine abiding by rules of the state, drivers will no longer be distracted and will be able to multi-task better instead, and transportation overall will be more efficient. Being a supporter of technology and fighting for new innovations, the advantages of a self-driving car outweighs the disadvantages in my opinion. Technological advances are occurring at such a fast pace that automobiles need to adapt and keep up. It is understandable that people are hesitant to this idea, as there are some major concerns that need to be addressed before being implemented. 

Jobs such as driver's ed instructors, DMV tellers and employees of companies like Uber and Lyft, will definitely suffer. As self-driving cars increase, the demand for their jobs will decrease and the unemployment rate will rise. Although this is an important concern, technology failure (i.e. glitches), would be an even greater concern. Accidents could be more fatal and insurance rates might increase to account for that. It would not be surprising if insurance companies created more "necessary" packages of coverage for autonomous cars. Instead of purchasing the normal auto insurance, there would be a greater domain for insurance companies to charge for and the limit for them would be endless. Auto insurance companies take tons of money anyway, what is another $200-300 per month? 

Overall, self-driving cars are infiltrating our society at a rather rapid rate. There are many positives and negatives about this advancement, yet there is still a need for more research and answers. As someone who personally hates driving and fears being in an accident, I will gladly take a self-driving car over a regular car any day.

A machine differs from the brain because it has a set algorithm programmed to provide an expected solution. There is no wrong solution as the only appropriate solution is already being used. Self-driving cars can help reduce accidents caused by humans since they do not have an inclination to be distracted. The machine has one job and it does it efficiently. As a result, drivers will no longer be distracted when multi-tasking. In addition to reducing human error, self-driving cars will provide more opportunities by providing transportation to those who cannot drive.

When Back to the Future I and II were released in the 80's, many people were captivated by the technology showed throughout the films. Even though technology has greatly advanced within the last decade, it still has not reached the status of flying cars and floating hover boards. As we inch closer to 2020, the idea of having such technology does not seem impossible anymore. Olivier Garret stated in a Forbes article that he believed 10 million self-driving cars will be on the road by 2020, and one out of four cars will be self-driving by 2030. Although Back to the Future II predicted cars flying in 2015, the estimate of 2020 for self-driving cars is not that far off.

Within the past year, companies like Ford, Honda, BMW and Tesla are few among the many automakers that are pushing for self-driving technology. This makes complete sense as technology is on an increasing incline forwards and will continue to do so as long as there is a need for it. Since the idea of self-driving cars is new and there has not been a ton of research on it, it is important to understand the advantages and disadvantages before getting into an autonomous vehicle. Being a supporter of technology and fighting for new innovations, the advantages of a self-driving car outweigh the disadvantages in my opinion, promising a safer, more efficient, and ultimately more productive future for transportation.

The most compelling advantage of autonomous vehicles lies in their potential to drastically reduce human error, which is currently responsible for over 90% of all traffic accidents. Unlike human drivers, self-driving cars do not get distracted, fatigued, or operate under the influence of alcohol or drugs. Equipped with an array of sensors, cameras, and radar, these vehicles maintain a constant 360-degree awareness of their surroundings, reacting faster and more consistently than any human. For instance, data from Waymo's extensive testing in various cities demonstrates a significantly lower accident rate compared to human-driven vehicles, with most incidents involving human drivers striking the autonomous car. This adherence to traffic laws and elimination of human fallibility promises a future with fewer collisions, injuries, and fatalities, transforming the safety landscape of our roads.

Beyond safety, self-driving cars offer unprecedented levels of efficiency and convenience. Drivers will no longer be mere operators but rather passengers, freed to multi-task, work, socialize, or simply relax during their commute. This reclaimed time represents a significant boost to personal productivity and quality of life. Furthermore, autonomous vehicles can optimize traffic flow through vehicle-to-vehicle communication and intelligent routing, reducing congestion and travel times. Imagine a city where cars seamlessly coordinate their movements, eliminating stop-and-go traffic and minimizing wasted fuel. This optimization not only benefits individual commuters but also has broader economic and environmental implications, making transportation overall more efficient and sustainable.

However, it is understandable that people are hesitant to this idea, as there are some major concerns that need to be addressed before widespread implementation. One significant challenge is the potential for job displacement. As self-driving cars become commonplace, the demand for human drivers in sectors like trucking, ride-sharing (Uber and Lyft), and even public transportation will inevitably decrease. Jobs such as driver's ed instructors and DMV tellers will also likely suffer. Although this is an important concern, it is a societal challenge that, while difficult, mirrors past technological revolutions where new industries and job roles emerged to replace those made obsolete. Investment in retraining programs and fostering innovation in related fields will be crucial to mitigate the impact on the workforce.

Another substantial concern revolves around technology failure and liability. The idea of a glitch in an autonomous system leading to a fatal accident raises profound questions about trust and accountability. While self-driving systems are rigorously tested, no technology is entirely infallible, and the potential for software bugs, hardware malfunctions, or even cyberattacks remains. Accidents, though potentially less frequent, could be more fatal in certain scenarios, and determining legal liability—whether with the car manufacturer, software provider, or owner—becomes complex. It would not be surprising if insurance companies created more "necessary" packages of coverage for autonomous cars, potentially increasing insurance rates to account for these novel risks and legal ambiguities. Addressing these concerns requires robust regulatory frameworks, transparent safety standards, and continuous development of fail-safe systems and ethical AI programming.

Overall, self-driving cars are infiltrating our society at a rather rapid rate, poised to revolutionize personal mobility. While critical concerns regarding job displacement and technological reliability demand careful consideration and proactive solutions, the profound benefits of enhanced safety, increased efficiency, and improved accessibility far outweigh these challenges. As humanity continues its relentless pursuit of innovation, the autonomous vehicle represents not just a new mode of transport, but a significant step towards a safer, more productive, and more integrated future. Embracing this technological evolution, while diligently addressing its complexities, will pave the way for a transformative era in how we move and interact with our world.

Self-driving cars are here. They have been piloted in Silicone Valley, San Francisco, New Mexico, Pittsburgh, Moscow, and other places. However, although the technology is here, they are not yet a part of people's lives. The cars are not yet able to drive in all weather conditions or handle anomalous traffic events, like if a storm knocks out traffic lights. Despite these ongoing hurdles self-driving cars have yet to overcome, the training of their neural networks pushes forward because once the technology can be implemented, the cars can solve real-world problems. The cars can make it safe for people with disabilities to run errands without needing to call a friend or a cab. They will reduce the number of traffic fatalities, especially those caused by driver inattention or inebriation. There are plenty of valid concerns about rolling self-driving cars into the market today but tomorrow is another story. Their use will ultimately benefit their passengers and the environment by creating a safer world.

The world is on the brink of environmental disaster. Irreparable damage has already be done, but further harm can be minimized through the advancement of many technologies, self-driving cars are one. Beyond the greater fuel efficiency self-driving cars provide, there is one feature that people do not often consider. Self-driving cars do not need to be owned by an individual. They can be owned by company, such as Zip Car or Uber. Consumers can pay these companies a monthly fee to have a car available to them whenever they need it and they can also opt in to a car pool so that the car picks up others going to the same place they are. Many people drive a car to work and home again, but they do not use the car throughout the day and they certainly don't use it while they are asleep. During times of non-use, the car could be picking up other riders. This would reduce the number of cars on the road, parked on the sides of streets, and adding to the carbon footprint.

There are valid concerns to this. People worry about the loss of jobs associated with self-driving cars as taxi drivers and truck drivers may become a job of the past. However, the job market is constantly evolving. It was not long ago when cars did not exist, ergo these jobs did not exist. The future will bring new fields for people to work in and new streams of revenue. Change is daunting, and many people are nervous about how a world with self-driving cars will function, but there is no question that it will function. People will adapt, as they did to the actual process of driving cars, and the world will be safer for it.
draft3:
Self-driving cars have arrived and are on the market even if their drivers wouldn't define them as such. Drivers are using cars that will auto park, brake if they approach an obstruction, and follow a car in front of them. They may not feel like they are self-driving as they do still require some human interaction and drivers can opt-in to such features, but when they are all used in combination, the car is in effect driving itself. Even when not in control of the wheel, these features can alert drivers to cars in their blind spot and provide a rear view when the driver is in moving backwards. Whether or not people want this future is now irrelevant; it's coming and soon people will be so used to it that they won’t remember what it was like to drive without a little backup support. Eventually, they won’t remember what it was like to drive at all, and that’s a good thing. Everyone will be safer when computers take the wheel.
	People are terrible drivers. We as humans like to believe that we can stay awake, pay attention, and maneuver an enormous hunk of metal at high speeds. The reality is that even when focused on the road, the human brain can only absorb a limited amount of its surroundings. People worry that self-driving cars are not as good as humans at detecting pedestrians or other anomalies, and they aren't as good--they're better at it. Computers have the capability to process the entire environment, not just what's in their fovea or what's left over after the brain has filtered out what it considers irrelevant. And they do it without getting drowsy or lost in their thoughts. Self-driving cars have years of clocked hours on the road. In all of those hours, only one pedestrian fatality has been caused by a self-driving car in the United States. On the other hand, 23 cyclists were killed in 2017 by human-driven cars in New York City alone (CBS New York). Clearly self-driving cars already have a better track record than their predecessors. 
	However, people should not be surrendering unmonitored control of the driving to their car just yet. There are some edges to human cognition that self-driving cars do not currently share even if they surpass it on others. One of them is the knowledge that some caution should be taken when accelerating around turns or at the peak of hills. I have been in cars that are set to regulate their speed to conditions of the road and the drivers around them, but on a nice day with no other cars in sight of the cameras, they will ramp up speed as they reach the peak of the hill without any knowledge of what may be on the other side. Due to the superior reaction times of a computer as compared to a human, the car could likely avoid any unforeseen obstruction that was just at the top and out of sight of the hill, but its passengers would not have enjoyed the abrupt stop. Even without an accident, such an abrupt stop can cause air bags to deploy or cause the body to jerk against the seatbelt and snap the collar bone. Such a need for abrupt stops would have been eliminated by a careful human driver who slowed down in anticipation of what might be lurking just beyond the crest of the hill. 
	The fact that self-driving cars are not ready to chauffeur people around en masse yet does not mean that it’s not coming though or that this reality is anything to fear. These cars just need more training, and unlike people, when one car trains, they all learn. Whether its navigating the Pittsburgh left or learning to be cautious when its camera does not detect straight road ahead, when one car learns, they all learn because they all share the technology. Thankfully, for the consumer, the transition to a self-driving car is a slow one. People will have time to adjust to the new reality and the software engineers will use this time to improve the technology. We have moved from basic cruise control to cars that will match pace with traffic. We have moved from rear-view cameras to cars that parallel park themselves. People are feeling safer about letting their car take over and when consumers are ready to give up the wheel, the car manufacturers will be there with a self-driving version. When that day comes, we’ll all be safer for it, and maybe the number of cyclists killed in New York City in a given year will finally reach zero. 
	CBSNewYork. “NYC Pedestrian Deaths Drop 32 Percent Last Year.” CBS New York, CBS New York, 8 Jan. 2018, newyork.cbslocal.com/2018/01/08/2017-pedestrian-deaths-nyc/.

Autonomous vehicles, however, are programmed to adhere to traffic laws, maintain constant vigilance, and react with split-second precision, eliminating these common human frailties. This technological precision promises a significant reduction in the nearly 1.35 million traffic fatalities globally each year, transforming our roads into far safer environments for everyone. Furthermore, self-driving technology offers unparalleled freedom to individuals currently limited by physical constraints or age. For the elderly who can no longer drive, or those with disabilities who rely on others for transportation, self-driving cars represent a gateway to independence. Imagine the elderly being able to visit doctors or friends without burdening family, or a visually impaired person confidently commuting to work. This enhanced mobility not only improves individual quality of life but also fosters greater societal inclusion, allowing more people to participate fully in economic and social spheres.

The world is on the brink of environmental disaster. Irreparable damage has already be done, but further harm can be minimized through the advancement of many technologies, self-driving cars are one. Beyond the greater fuel efficiency self-driving cars provide, there is one feature that people do not often consider. Self-driving cars do not need to be owned by an individual. They can be owned by company, such as Zip Car or Uber. Consumers can pay these companies a monthly fee to have a car available to them whenever they need it and they can also opt in to a car pool so that the car picks up others going to the same place they are. Many people drive a car to work and home again, but they do not use the car throughout the day and they certainly don't use it while they are asleep. During times of non-use, the car could be picking up other riders. This would reduce the number of cars on the road, parked on the sides of streets, and adding to the carbon footprint.

There are valid concerns to this. People worry about the loss of jobs associated with self-driving cars as taxi drivers and truck drivers may become a job of the past. However, the job market is constantly evolving. It was not long ago when cars did not exist, ergo these jobs did not exist. The future will bring new fields for people to work in and new streams of revenue. Change is daunting, and many people are nervous about how a world with self-driving cars will function, but there is no question that it will function. People will adapt, as they did to the actual process of driving cars, and the world will be safer for it.

In conclusion, while the journey to fully autonomous vehicles presents undeniable challenges, the overwhelming benefits they offer to society are too significant to ignore. From drastically reducing traffic fatalities and empowering individuals with disabilities to fostering a more sustainable, environmentally friendly transportation system, self-driving cars stand poised to revolutionize our daily lives. The concerns surrounding job displacement, though valid, are part of the natural evolution of technology and the economy, which historically adapts and creates new opportunities. As the technology continues to mature and overcome its remaining hurdles, the vision of a safer, more accessible, and greener future driven by autonomous vehicles moves closer to reality, promising a transformative impact on humanity.

output_text:
The 21st century has witnessed an unprecedented surge in technological advancement and automation. Fueled by high computing power and sophisticated algorithms, humans have successfully automated countless tasks. Driving, a skill that is a passion for some and a mundane chore for others, has long been a fundamental part of daily life. While improvements in road infrastructure have somewhat eased the driving experience, the prospect of self-driving cars promises to eliminate this daily "computation" altogether. This technology could certainly alleviate the burden for professional drivers, for whom driving is a job rather than a choice. However, while I strongly support continued research into autonomous vehicles, I contend that their widespread implementation at this stage is premature and fraught with significant challenges.

While self-driving cars promise to reduce the effort of driving, their widespread applicability is severely limited by current technological and infrastructural constraints. Many, particularly in developed nations, have grown up with the convenience of reliable roads, but such conditions are far from a universal experience. My own upbringing in the foothills of the Himalayas, for instance, exposed me to treacherous curves and challenging terrains where constant human concentration is paramount. While autonomous vehicles have shown impressive results in controlled environments like the United States, renowned for its superior infrastructure, such conditions are not globally representative. The efficacy of self-driving technology hinges on highly accurate GPS mapping systems, which are neither universally available nor consistently reliable in adverse weather conditions. Furthermore, the machine learning algorithms powering these vehicles, while advanced, cannot yet guarantee 100% accuracy in all unforeseen circumstances, raising significant safety concerns in unpredictable real-world scenarios.

Beyond technological limitations, the human element and financial realities present additional hurdles to widespread adoption. Despite advancements, many individuals, particularly older generations, remain hesitant to fully trust or operate highly sophisticated autonomous systems. This reluctance to cede control to a machine, or a lack of familiarity with complex interfaces, could paradoxically lead to human error during critical moments, potentially exacerbating dangerous situations rather than preventing them. Moreover, even if these human-interface challenges were resolved, the sheer financial investment required for large-scale implementation is staggering. The cost of manufacturing these advanced vehicles, coupled with the immense expense of upgrading and maintaining the necessary road infrastructure globally, currently far outweighs the projected utility and economic benefits of a complete shift to autonomous driving.

The much-touted promise of self-driving cars to drastically reduce accidents has, in recent times, been met with growing skepticism. While proponents highlight their potential, numerous incidents involving autonomous vehicles have demonstrated that they do not entirely eliminate the risk of collisions. A single machine malfunction, whether due to software glitches or sensor failures, can lead to critical issues. In such scenarios, the human operator, accustomed to less active involvement, might find it challenging to quickly diagnose the problem and regain full manual control, potentially leading to severe accidents in mere seconds. This risk is particularly acute in environments like the demanding Himalayan roads, where a driver's constant, undivided attention is essential; any autonomous system malfunction there could prove catastrophic.

In conclusion, while the concept of self-driving vehicles holds immense promise and warrants continued research, their widespread implementation faces formidable obstacles. These challenges encompass inadequate global infrastructure, unreliable GPS availability, vulnerability to diverse weather conditions, significant financial investment, and lingering concerns about human interaction and system reliability. Therefore, a universal rollout of autonomous vehicles is currently not a feasible or responsible endeavor. While targeted application in highly controlled environments where all constraints can be readily met might be viable, the dream of a fully self-driving world remains distant, requiring substantial advancements and careful consideration before it can truly become a safe and equitable reality.

Self-driving vehicles will decrease fatalities, shave time off commutes, decrease the amount of money being spent on mass transit, and provide more options of transportation for the disabled. At least one of these options directly relates to each, and every person in a given community. Many issues that plague individuals revolve around transportation. From having to wake up earlier in the day because of an increased workload to compensate for traffic, to getting a call that a loved one was in an accident. The increased amount of self-driving vehicles being adopted by consumers will have a positive correlation with the daily lives of those that do not own them.

Currently, the U.S. Department of Transportation has assessed the value of a life at $9.2 million. By way of safer roads through self-driving vehicles being more prevalent, because of factors such as the reduction of human error through computers doing the driving, the amount of money being spent by insurance companies will drive down insurance premiums so they can remain competitive. As these vehicles become more common on the roads the cost to purchase them will go down. Companies will find ways to lower costs through more efficient methods of production, and distribution. The savings of money is then passed down to local, and state governments. Less money will have to be spent on methods of mass transit. City buses, and trains maintenance costs will plummet, and those savings are then passed down to the tax paying citizens of the said cities, and states.

A caveat of self-driving vehicles is the effect that it will have on some employers, a legitimate concern for many. The plentiful amount of vehicles which will transport its occupants will decrease the need for public transportation, and taxi services, undoubtedly leading to a reduction in certain driving-related fields. However, this displacement will be offset by the creation of numerous new opportunities. An increased need for engineers to design and refine autonomous systems, specialized auto mechanics to maintain complex vehicle technology, road repairmen/women to adapt infrastructure for smart vehicles, cybersecurity experts to protect systems, and salespeople to integrate these vehicles into the market will have a profound impact on economies. This shift represents an evolution in the workforce, creating higher-skilled jobs and fostering innovation rather than a net loss of employment.

Beyond safety and economic shifts, self-driving cars promise to shave significant time off daily commutes, thereby enhancing personal productivity and quality of life. Autonomous vehicles, unburdened by human distractions or fatigue, can maintain optimal speeds and following distances, leading to smoother traffic flow and reduced congestion. Furthermore, advanced AI-driven navigation systems can dynamically reroute vehicles to avoid bottlenecks, minimizing delays that currently plague human-driven commutes. This saved time is not merely a reduction in sitting idle; it allows individuals to reclaim precious hours for work, education, family, or leisure activities, transforming what was once unproductive travel into valuable personal time. The ability for vehicles to drop off passengers and then self-park or even return home eliminates the time-consuming search for parking, further streamlining the daily routine.

Perhaps one of the most transformative impacts of self-driving vehicles will be the expanded transportation options for individuals with disabilities. For many, mobility is severely limited by the need for a human driver, specialized public transit, or expensive adapted vehicles. Autonomous cars remove these barriers entirely. An individual with a physical disability could summon a vehicle tailored to their specific needs – perhaps with an automated ramp for a wheelchair – and travel independently to work, appointments, or social events without reliance on caregivers or public transport schedules. This newfound independence will not only improve their daily convenience but also grant greater access to employment, education, and social participation, significantly enhancing their quality of life and integration into the community. The on-demand nature and potential cost-effectiveness of these services would make reliable transportation accessible to a population often marginalized by current systems.

The supporters and people that oppose self-driving vehicles both have valid concerns for their positions. But, if there can be a value put on a human life then isn’t there a way that we can save on those costs by mitigating the instances of death? Self-driving vehicles is the way to make that come to fruition. The constant development of safety measures in the autonomous systems will get better as consumers adopt more of the vehicles. Accounts of drunk, tired, and distracted driving will continuously plummet. Therefore, making the roads safer for us all, while simultaneously improving economic efficiency, reducing commute times, and providing unprecedented mobility for the disabled. The widespread adoption of self-driving vehicles represents not just a technological leap, but a societal transformation poised to profoundly improve the quality of life for individuals and communities across the globe.

draft1:
The article "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated With Self-Driving Cars", provides an in depth look into the reasoning of whether or not we, as a society, should adopt self-driving vehicles.  Of the 20 "pros" listed, an increase in safety was likely the leading factor, but also included were the benefits in commute times and city travel, the possibility for the driver to do something productive in the time that would otherwise be used for driving, and the idea that elderly or disabled people would be able to ride self-driving cars without worry.  However, in contrast to the advantages listed in the article, the author also offered 20 disadvantages.  Many of these focused on the training involved in learning how to operate it, the high cost, security issues (both personal and cybersecurity), legal ramifications, the fact that a self-driving vehicle will not be able to appropriately respond to all situations and other technical hangups,and the financial implications caused by uprooting an old industry. 

When weighing the "pros" and "cons" in order to gauge whether adopting self-driving cars would be beneficial to society, it is important to look at the whole picture.  The "pros" are all valid, while it appears that many of the "cons" can be addressed as the self-driving vehicles further develop.  For example, the high initial cost is cited as a reason against adopting these vehicles.  However, what isn't taken into consideration in this case is the fact that new technologies typically drastically drop in price over a short amount of time.  We often hear the story of the first computers being the size of an entire room and costing hundreds of thousands of dollars at the time.  However, in a few tens of years the price of those computers came down enough that many families could afford them.  I believe a similar situation could occur with the sensor and computer package required in self-driving vehicles.  As a second example, cybersecurity has become a very hot topic recently and appears in the "cons" against these vehicles.  However, several very critical pieces of infrastructure are operated and they have been largely protected against cybersecurity attacks.  Take for example remote controlled drones currently in use by the military.  The risk of a cyber attack is definitely present, but the designers have found a way to adequately protect the devices.  Something similar could be done for self-driving vehicles.

Although several more examples supporting the idea that many of the present "cons" seen in self-driving vehicle adoption can be corrected in the future, it is unnecessary seeing that they are quite similar.  However, what is important to notice is that all of the "pros" do not seem as if they will be changing, even as the vehicles themselves improve.  Therefore I recommend the gradual adoption of self-driving vehicles into society.

Take for example remote controlled drones currently in use by the military. The risk of a cyber attack is definitely present, but the designers have found a way to adequately protect the devices. Something similar could be done for self-driving vehicles.

Although several more examples supporting the idea that many of the present "cons" seen in self-driving vehicle adoption can be corrected in the future, it is unnecessary seeing that they are quite similar. However, what is important to notice is that all of the "pros" do not seem as if they will be changing, even as the vehicles themselves improve. Therefore I recommend the gradual adoption of self-driving vehicles into society.

The enduring nature of these "pros" forms the bedrock of the argument for embracing this technology. The most compelling benefit, increased safety, stands as a constant. Human error accounts for a vast majority of traffic accidents, leading to countless injuries and fatalities annually. Self-driving vehicles, with their consistent adherence to traffic laws, absence of distraction, and faster reaction times, offer the promise of drastically reducing these tragic statistics. This inherent advantage is not subject to technological obsolescence but rather will be enhanced with every iteration of improved AI and sensor technology. Similarly, the potential for vastly improved commute times and the opportunity for drivers to engage in productive activities rather than focusing on the road presents a massive societal gain. This reclaims countless hours that can be dedicated to work, education, or personal well-being, fundamentally altering our daily routines for the better. Furthermore, the accessibility offered to the elderly and disabled population is a profound humanitarian benefit, granting independence and mobility to those who might otherwise be constrained, thereby enriching their lives and fostering greater social inclusion.

While many immediate "cons" are temporary and addressable through technological advancement, it is important to acknowledge that some complex challenges, such as establishing clear legal frameworks for liability in accidents or navigating ethical dilemmas in unavoidable crash scenarios, will require ongoing societal discussion and careful regulation. However, these are not insurmountable barriers. A strategy of gradual adoption, encompassing pilot programs, phased rollouts, and continuous legislative updates, can effectively manage and overcome these complexities as the technology matures and public understanding evolves. The profound and enduring benefits of self-driving cars—the potential to save lives, boost economic productivity, and enhance the quality of life for millions—far outweigh these manageable long-term challenges, especially when weighed against the existing problems inherent in human-driven transportation.

In conclusion, the detailed analysis of the "pros" and "cons" of self-driving vehicles reveals a compelling case for their integration into society. While initial hurdles such as high cost and cybersecurity concerns are legitimate, history demonstrates that such issues are typically transient, diminishing significantly with technological advancement and widespread adoption. Conversely, the core advantages—enhanced safety, increased productivity, and greater accessibility—are fundamental, enduring benefits that promise to transform society for the better. By carefully navigating the remaining complex challenges through a measured, gradual adoption strategy, we can unlock the immense potential of self-driving technology, ushering in an era of safer, more efficient, and more inclusive transportation for all.

output_text:
The advent of self-driving cars presents a transformative shift in transportation, promising both significant advantages and notable disadvantages. While concerns regarding cost, job displacement, and technical reliability are valid, the potential benefits, particularly in enhancing safety and efficiency, strongly position autonomous vehicles as a net positive for modern society. This essay will argue that self-driving cars offer a crucial pathway to reducing traffic fatalities, improving urban mobility, and fostering a more productive environment, ultimately outweighing their drawbacks.

One of the most compelling advantages of self-driving cars lies in their potential to dramatically decrease the percentage of car crashes and, consequently, save countless lives. Human error is a primary factor in the vast majority of accidents, a factor largely eliminated by autonomous systems. According to the U.S. Department of Transportation, the economic value of a human life, estimated at over $9 million, translates into significant cost savings in insurance and healthcare associated with accident recovery alone. Research from consulting firms like McKinsey & Company in 2015 indicated that self-driving cars could reduce traffic fatalities by 90% once fully deployed. This is particularly significant given that motor vehicle crashes remain a leading cause of death in the United States, with over 37,000 fatalities reported by the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration in 2016 alone. By mitigating human fallibility, self-driving cars offer the most promising solution to curb this devastating loss of life.

Beyond safety, self-driving cars promise to revolutionize daily commutes by enhancing efficiency and productivity. These vehicles are designed to optimize traffic flow, potentially eliminating congestion and significantly reducing commute times for drivers in high-traffic areas. This efficiency would also lead to less wasted gasoline, offering environmental and economic benefits. Furthermore, autonomous vehicles allow occupants to complete other tasks while in transit, transforming unproductive travel time into opportunities for work, relaxation, or personal development. For larger cities, this technology could mean the provision of more reliable and accessible transportation options for residents, easing the burden on existing infrastructure and public services.

While the benefits are substantial, it is important to acknowledge the disadvantages associated with widespread adoption of self-driving cars. A primary concern is affordability; the high initial cost of these vehicles may render them inaccessible to low-income individuals, creating a potential divide in transportation access. Another significant challenge is the projected loss of jobs in the transportation sector, particularly for freight drivers and taxi operators, which could negatively impact unemployment rates and the economy. Concerns also exist about the reliability of sensor technology in adverse weather conditions, such as heavy rain, and the potential for drivers to lose the skill of operating a vehicle manually. However, these challenges, while real, can be mitigated through policy, retraining programs, and ongoing technological advancements. The societal gains in safety and efficiency, coupled with the long-term economic benefits from reduced accidents and increased productivity, outweigh these transitional difficulties.

The societal impact of self-driving cars extends beyond immediate safety and efficiency. One less-discussed benefit is the potential to decrease police activity related to traffic enforcement. In today's society, a simple traffic violation can sometimes escalate into serious confrontations with law enforcement. By largely eliminating human-driven traffic infractions, autonomous vehicles could significantly reduce these interactions, fostering a safer and less contentious environment on the roads for both citizens and law enforcement. Moreover, the reduced need for driver's license issuance could lead to a decrease in administrative costs, potentially translating into lower taxes. The continuous improvement in the mechanics, accuracy, and algorithms of self-driving cars since their inception further solidifies their readiness for widespread integration and their capacity to address these broader societal challenges.

In conclusion, self-driving cars represent a pivotal technological advancement with the capacity to profoundly improve society. While the transition to an autonomous future will undoubtedly present hurdles, including initial costs, job displacement, and technological integration challenges, the overwhelming advantages in terms of safety, efficiency, and broader societal benefits are undeniable. The ability to drastically reduce traffic fatalities, enhance urban mobility, increase productivity, and even mitigate potential negative interactions with law enforcement positions self-driving cars as a crucial innovation. As technology continues to evolve, addressing current limitations, the benefits of self-driving cars will increasingly outweigh their disadvantages, making them an indispensable component of our future transportation landscape. I am firmly on the side of embracing this transformative technology for the betterment of society.

The new technology of self-driving cars is decades in the making in Pittsburgh, with Carnegie Mellon first leading the way in the 1990s.  Since then the city has been a literal testing ground for companies like Uber and Argo AI, with the mayor keen on attracting (and keeping) talent at the forefront of this technology.  Reactions have long been mixed, though, even before a fatal accident in Arizona recently halted Uber automated test drives.  The strong opposition to automated vehicles in the aftermath, however, is somewhat surprising, given how much safer the technology tends to be.  Or, rather, how unsafe human drivers actually are.

A single death at the hands of an automated Uber stopped its vehicle testing, yet eighty-one percent of vehicular accidents are the result of human error.  Those in working in Oakland routinely see that this human error extends to pedestrians and cyclists in addition to human drivers.  Humans lack the reaction time to respond to unexpected changes in scenery, from a pedestrian stepping out onto Forbes to a car swerving abruptly to miss a pothole.  Human drivers are also increasingly distracted, and are choosing to distract themselves with a dizzying array of activities from personal grooming to streaming television.  Self-driving cars also solve the problem of drunk driving, as autonomous vehicles double as designated drivers.  In a city with limited taxi service and practically no commercial designated driving companies, drunk drivers find themselves tempted to drive from the Southside to the South Hills with often destructive consequences.

Accessibility and inclusion are on residents' minds, too, as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system that will make current connections faster but will leave lower income and disabled locals out of the discussion.  Fleets of self-driving cars would not only ferry drunk passengers but others without access to vehicles as well, including those in neighborhoods lacking in public transportation.  Pittsburgh's elderly and disabled, who used to be better served by paratransit services before budget cuts gutted these systems, are especially vulnerable by their lack of access.

The counter to the accessibility argument is, of course, the high cost of self-driving vehicles.  With an initial cost of more than $100,000, there is little overlap between the people who currently rely on public transportation and those who can immediately purchase self-driving vehicles.  Not many car owners could afford a $100,000 vehicle, for that matter.  This is alleviated by two considerations, though.  The cost of this technology will inevitably go down, just like the brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers of our youth.  What is unaffordable now may not be in a few years.  What is less certain, though, is whether the city can shed its car-first mentality and embrace shared self-driving vehicles, a public personal transit system.

Car makers have been introducing automated technology in baby steps, from rear-facing cameras to park-assist features and obstacle sensors.  Reactions have long been mixed, though, even before a fatal accident in Arizona recently halted Uber automated test drives.  What is surprising is the strong opposition to automated vehicles in the aftermath, given how much safer the technology tends to be. Or, rather, how unsafe human drivers actually are. This danger is not just limited to drivers, but to everyone in our neighborhoods: pedestrians, cyclists, and bus-riders alike. The increased safety of our roads for all residents that we will see with self-driving cars is the greatest reason to advocate for their use throughout the city. 
	A single death at the hands of an automated Uber stopped its vehicle testing, yet the Center for Disease Control estimates over 33,000 Americans are killed each year by automobiles.  An alarming  81% of these vehicular accidents are the result of human error. Those working in Oakland routinely see that this human error extends to pedestrians and cyclists in addition to human drivers. Humans lack the reaction time to respond to unexpected changes in scenery, from a pedestrian stepping out onto Forbes to a car swerving abruptly to miss a pothole. Human drivers are also increasingly distracted, and are choosing to distract themselves with an obscene array of activities from personal grooming to streaming television. For those distracted and inhibited by alcohol, self-driving cars also solve the problem of drunk driving, as autonomous vehicles double as designated drivers. In a city with limited taxi service and practically no private designated driving companies, drunk drivers find themselves tempted to make the drive from the Southside to the South Hills, with often destructive consequences. 
	Accessibility and inclusion are on residents' minds, too, as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system that will make current connections faster but will leave lower-income and disabled locals on the curb. Fleets of self-driving cars would not only ferry drunk passengers but others without access to vehicles as well, including those in neighborhoods lacking in public transportation. Pittsburgh's elderly and disabled, who used to be better served by paratransit services before budget cuts gutted these systems, are especially vulnerable by their lack of access. 
	The counter to the accessibility argument is, of course, the high cost of self-driving vehicles. With an initial outlay of more than $100,000, there is little overlap between the people who currently rely on public transportation and those who can immediately purchase self-driving vehicles. Not many car owners could afford a $100,000 vehicle, for that matter. This is alleviated by two considerations, though. The cost of this technology will inevitably go down, just like the brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers of our youth. What is unaffordable now may not be in a few years. What is less certain, though, is whether the city can shed its car-first mentality and embrace shared self-driving vehicles, a public personal transit system.  Uber and Lyft have revolutionized Pittsburgh's taxi scene in just a few years, after all, and those improvements to accessibility and availability will improve as the numbers of self-driving cars increase.
	Few weeks go by where out city is not making national headlines for its livability, its technology, and its overall rust-belt renaissance. Self-driving cars are currently at the vanguard of this movement, and both attract talent to the region and retain the minds trained by Carnegie Mellon and Pitt. While this has a cachet of modernity and elitism, the benefits of self-driving cars reach everyone. Indeed, pockets of our city now under served and under represented will better enjoy the benefits of our "rust belt renaissance."  When people really look at the costs of human drivers---and human errors---the choice to adapt self-driving cars throughout the city will be an easy one.
