draft1:
It seems that cars have always been the same: four wheels, a body, a steering wheel. They are ridiculously simple! Compare to the history of bicycles for example. If you happen to have seen those old black and white photographs of early bicycles, you will see a complex evolution, akin to those in biology! Nevertheless, something on the horizon is going to turn everything about cars upside down (well, the cars themselves will probably still remain on their four wheels, though!)

Self-driving cars are already among us fueling the debate over their good and and evil. Proponents of automated cars argue that self-driving cars will eliminate the catastrophic number of fatal car crashes because the cold-blooded machines will not be distracted by music, a passer-by, or be under the influence of alcohol and drugs. The other feature they cite is the possibility for many, or even all, cars in a certain city to communicate with one another to coordinate the speed levels, and red light waits so as to achieve the maximum flow and minimum travel times. Automated cars, they say, will allow for more cars to drive next to each other, due to their precision in maneuvering, and yet lead to less congestion. Another major advantage will be their service to the disabled and elderly people. Once a good proportion of cars on the street become self-drivers, the advocates claim that the whole society will benefit: saving lives, avoiding financial setbacks from car accidents, less fuel consumption, fewer traffic police officers on duty, etc. 

However, the opponents of the technology have some legitimate concerns. In this category there are some superficial and trivial arguments that can be refuted right away. For example, the phobia of a scenario where a car breaks down and the human driver, having forgotten how to drive, has no idea of what to do, leading to chaos in the city cannot be a real argument against self-driving cars. Already today, an average individual has no skill to get a car to start up if it all of a sudden stops in the middle of street. Such a situation rarely disrupts a whole city! Some other arguments question the limits of the technology. What if it snows? What if the road is full of potholes (such as those in Pittsburgh!)? Well, these while valid concerns do not prove the technology insufficient. There are improvements coming every day and all those conditions will be taken care of.

With the obvious criticism of the self-driving cars out of way, we arrive at some deep issues against the technology. On the infrastructure level, there is the problem of providing cyber security to such a delicate network of interconnected software that store and communicate data between cars. If, as Guardian suggests, there is a potential for hackers to manipulate such a system, then it will be a true disaster. Achieving a high level of security while possible could be prohibitively costly, overshadowing any amount of saving on gasoline.

However, the biggest challenge facing self-driving cars is their popularity. The pros listed above all are predicated on the hope that they will come in large quantities. The effect of having cars coordinate their paths will not be felt if they substitute only 2% of all the cars out there. Same holds true for safety. Have you noticed that sometimes, ironically, the safest drivers, are the very cause of some accidents? Imagine a road where the speed limit is 50, but "everyone knows that it is ok to go 60 (when the police is not around!)". Then that one guy who sticks to that 50 mph limit is the source of frustration for those behind him/her and a possible danger for everyone involved. Thus, the advantages of autonomous cars will begin to take effect only after a good portion of the society adopts them.

But with the currently high cost of these cars, and the natural public resistance to change, it seems that self-driving cars will, for the coming years, live in the margins, only triggering imagination and debates from time to time. At this point in time, we are just not ready yet to embrace them -- definitely not in large numbers.

Bicycles have seen an evolution akin to those in biology, starting with extremely disproportionate wheels, experimenting with bizarre rider positions, ... Cars, on the other hand, have always been the same: four same-sized wheels, a bumpy body, doors, windshield, and a steering wheel. However, something on the horizon might turn everything about cars upside down: self-driving cars. 
Self-driving cars are automobiles equipped with sophisticated technology that allows them to navigate with no interference from a human driver -- think a car with an invisible driver! Several models, including one from Google, have been tested on the streets of California with success, which settles the question from the engineering and technological point of view and fuels the debate about their promises and threats to the society.
	Proponents of automated cars argue that self-driving cars will eliminate the stunning number of fatal car crashes, because the cold-blooded machines will not be distracted by music, a passer-by, or be under the influence of alcohol and drugs. The other feature they cite is the possibility for many, or even all, cars in a certain city to communicate with one another to coordinate their speeds, red light waits,etc so as to achieve the maximum flow and minimum travel times. Automated cars, they say, will allow for more cars to drive next to each other, due to their precision in maneuvering, and yet lead to less congestion. Another major advantage will be their service to the disabled and elderly people. Once a good proportion of cars on the street become self-drivers, the advocates claim that the whole society will benefit: saving lives, avoiding financial setbacks from car accidents, less fuel consumption, fewer traffic police officers on duty, etc. 
	As utopian as the technology seems, I have to side with the critics of the technology. There are several serious issues with the predictions above. 
	Firstly, self-driving cars will not necessarily be economical. There will be need for an extremely cyber secure and delicate network of interconnected software that store and communicate data between cars. If, as Guardian suggests, there is a potential for hackers to manipulate such a system, then it will be a true disaster. Achieving a high level of security could be prohibitively costly, overshadowing any amount of saving,say, on gasoline. The manufacturing of the cars will require complex software and hardware and production costs will be higher than the average today. Even with the autonomous cars driving accidents could and will occur. Maybe the number of accidents will be lower, but remember that the damage befallen on these expensive cars will be higher. There are other expenses we can foresee: staff that can repair the cars, customer service that can help out a baffled and helpless human passenger in a car,... Therefore, it is not clear whether self-driving cars will in fact be cost-efficient. 
	Secondly,  the pros listed for autonomous cars are all predicated on the hope that they will come in large quantities. In case this optimistic assumption is not realized, the benefits promised by the technology will not be delivered. For example, the effect of having cars coordinate their paths will not be felt if they substitute only 2% of all the cars out there. Same holds true for safety. Self-driving cars can reduce the number of accidents only if they make up a significant percentage of all traffic. A handful of them among human drivers could in fact lead to human misinterpretation of their behavior and lead to accidents. Imagine a driverless car passing by inches by your car at a high speed. It could be that the machine "knows" what it's doing, but what if the human driver makes the mistake under the panic he or she experiences? As we can see, the advantages of autonomous cars will begin to take effect only after a good portion of the society adopts them. But will the society in fact welcome them?
I do not believe they will. People have good reasons to not embrace the technology -- number one being their cost. Cars are already expensive and if the autonomous cars cannot compete with the traditional ones in terms of cost, they will remain a luxury item.  Other reasons may include people's natural phobia of drastic changes. We do hear people not willing to "give up the control" and "joy of driving". Such personal associations with cars and ethical issues (such as cars being programmed to minimize fatalities in an eventful situation by "directing the danger toward its own passengers") may turn out to be strong concerns for people that come in te way of transition to a driverless traffic.
	In summary, despite my praise for the engineering accomplishment, I do not believe that the society is ready for this revolution just yet. We may have to tolerate the boring junkers for a couple more decades.

It seems that cars have always been the same: four wheels, a body, a steering wheel. They are ridiculously simple! Compare to the history of bicycles for example. If you happen to have seen those old black and white photographs of early bicycles, you will see a complex evolution, akin to those in biology! Nevertheless, something on the horizon is going to turn everything about cars upside down: the advent of self-driving vehicles. While these autonomous cars promise revolutionary benefits in safety, efficiency, and accessibility, their widespread adoption faces formidable challenges related to infrastructure, cybersecurity, and public acceptance, making their immediate future as a dominant mode of transport uncertain.

Indeed, the proponents of self-driving cars champion a future where transportation is dramatically safer and more efficient. They argue that autonomous vehicles will virtually eliminate the catastrophic number of fatal car crashes, as these "cold-blooded machines" will not be distracted by music, a passer-by, or be under the influence of alcohol and drugs. Furthermore, advocates envision a seamless urban landscape where cars communicate with one another, coordinating speed levels and traffic light waits to achieve maximum flow and minimum travel times. This precision in maneuvering, they claim, will allow for more cars to drive in closer proximity, paradoxically leading to less congestion. Beyond efficiency, a major humanitarian advantage lies in their potential to provide unprecedented mobility for disabled and elderly individuals. The collective societal benefits, proponents suggest, are immense: saving lives, avoiding financial setbacks from accidents, reducing fuel consumption, and even decreasing the need for traffic police officers on duty.

While the potential benefits are compelling, the path to widespread self-driving car adoption is fraught with significant hurdles, particularly on the infrastructure level. A primary concern is the monumental task of providing robust cybersecurity for such a delicate network of interconnected software that stores and communicates sensitive data between vehicles. As publications like The Guardian have suggested, the potential for hackers to manipulate or cripple such a system presents a truly catastrophic scenario. Achieving the necessary high level of security, while technically possible, could prove prohibitively costly, potentially overshadowing any projected savings on fuel or accident-related expenses.

Beyond the technical and infrastructural demands, the biggest challenge facing self-driving cars is arguably their popularity and the need for widespread adoption. Many of the touted advantages, such as enhanced safety and coordinated traffic flow, are predicated on a critical mass of autonomous vehicles on the road. The intended effect of cars coordinating their paths, for instance, will not be realized if they constitute only a tiny fraction of the total vehicles. A similar dynamic applies to safety; a mixed fleet of human-driven and self-driven cars could even introduce new complexities. Consider a scenario where an autonomous vehicle rigidly adheres to the speed limit on a road where human drivers routinely exceed it. Such a disparity could create frustration and unexpected hazards for both human and machine drivers. Thus, the transformative benefits of autonomous cars will only begin to materialize once a significant portion of society embraces them.

However, with the currently high cost of these advanced vehicles and the natural public resistance to such a profound technological shift, widespread adoption remains a distant prospect. Integrating self-driving cars into existing infrastructure and societal norms presents a complex puzzle. Consequently, for the foreseeable future, self-driving cars are likely to remain a niche technology, primarily fueling academic debates and public imagination rather than dominating our roads. Until significant breakthroughs address these multifaceted challenges, society is simply not ready to embrace them on a large scale.

output_text:
Self-driving vehicles present a complex interplay of significant advantages and considerable disadvantages. While the potential for enhanced accessibility and safety holds immense promise for the future of transportation, the inherent risks associated with ceding control to artificial intelligence demand rigorous scrutiny and robust safeguards. Ultimately, despite the undeniable benefits, the current technological and ethical challenges suggest that widespread, fully autonomous vehicle adoption should proceed with cautious optimism and stringent regulation.

Among the most compelling advantages of self-driving technology is its capacity to democratize automobile transportation for individuals who might otherwise be unable to drive. This includes people with physical, mental, or emotional disabilities that prevent them from safely operating a vehicle, as well as the elderly who may experience diminished reflexes or vision. As the global population ages, particularly with the large baby boomer generation, self-driving cars offer a vital solution for maintaining independence and mobility. Furthermore, artificial intelligence excels at detecting risky situations, remaining alert to potential perils such as objects entering the vehicle's immediate field of vision. Drivers of self-driving cars would not need to worry about emergency situations like autonomous braking, for instance, because the computer is programmed to follow necessary steps to make split-second decisions to stop the car "on a dime," potentially reducing accidents caused by human error or delayed reactions.

Beyond safety, self-driving vehicles offer practical conveniences that enhance the driving experience. Perhaps the most universally appreciated skill in the self-driver's arsenal is automated parking. In today's increasingly crowded urban environments, parking spaces are becoming fewer and smaller, making the task a source of significant stress for many drivers. The nervousness and uncertainty that often accompany the thought or need to parallel park or maneuver into tight spots can be entirely remedied when the computer takes the wheel. Drivers can simply sit back and appreciate the precision and efficiency with which the computer has been programmed to execute this often-dreaded task, freeing up mental energy for other pursuits.

However, the very notion of submitting the full power of driving to a computer raises profound concerns. It is entirely possible for a computer system to malfunction, and such an occurrence could put not only the driver at risk but also other occupants of the vehicle and unsuspecting pedestrians. A malfunctioning computer might, in fact, create a more dangerous situation than if a human driver were at the controls, as human intuition and adaptability can sometimes compensate for unexpected scenarios in ways that even advanced algorithms cannot. This fundamental distrust is amplified by the potential for malicious intent. Robotic intricacies aside, the human element can wreak havoc in cases such as hacking, where terrorism or cyber-crime could commandeer vehicles, turning them into instruments of harm. The vulnerability of interconnected systems to external manipulation presents a significant security challenge that must be thoroughly addressed.

Moreover, the ethical dilemmas posed by autonomous vehicles are complex and largely unresolved. In unavoidable accident scenarios, how should a self-driving car be programmed to make life-or-death decisions? Should it prioritize the safety of its occupants, or minimize harm to the greatest number of people outside the vehicle? These "trolley problem" scenarios highlight the need for a robust ethical framework and transparent programming guidelines, which are still in their nascent stages of development. Without clear societal consensus and legal precedents, the widespread deployment of vehicles making such decisions could lead to significant moral and legal quandaries.

In conclusion, while self-driving cars hold immense promise for improving accessibility, enhancing safety, and offering unparalleled convenience, their widespread adoption hinges on overcoming significant hurdles. The benefits of increased mobility for the elderly and disabled, coupled with the potential reduction in accidents due to human error, are compelling. Yet, the critical risks of system malfunction, cyber-security threats, and unresolved ethical programming dilemmas cannot be understated. Therefore, a balanced approach is essential, one that fosters innovation while simultaneously demanding rigorous testing, robust regulatory oversight, and public discourse to ensure that the future of autonomous transportation prioritizes safety, security, and human well-being above all else.

Self-driving cars appear to be the next wave of technology that will impact our lives, following the pattern previously exploited by the internet, cell phones, and other technological advances. The prospect of cars that autonomously drive is an attractive one for a number of positive reasons. One argument for self-driving cars could be that they would reduce car accidents and therefore save lives. 81 percent of car crashes are the result of human error; therefore, it is appealing to think that computer systems specifically designed to combat the issues associated with human error could be good alternatives. By developing specific software algorithms to respond to a myriad of situations and scenarios, self-driving cars appear to have the capability to handle any situation a human driver might encounter. Because self-driving cars would be fully automated to respond to all situations, it is comforting to think that all actions made in response to situations would lead to the best possible outcomes.
	Unfortunately, we still have a way to go before these prospects and visions about self-driving cars can truly be attained. I would like to argue that we need to spend more time considering the legal and moral dilemmas presented by self-driving cars and their actions, and how these differ from the actions taken by typical human drivers. To exemplify a possible situation in which a legal and moral dilemma might arise, I would like to use a twist on the common ethical conundrum posed by the trolley problem. For our purposes, imagine you are sitting behind the wheel of your car and driving down the street. Seemingly out of nowhere, a ball rolls into the street, followed by a child chasing after it. In the moment in which you see the child, you must make a decision as to how you will react. Your two choices consist of either attempting to brake and hope that you stop in time to not injure the child, or swerving the car to the side of the road and risk hitting a tree, thus injuring yourself. As humans in this type of situation, we would be required to react nearly instantly, therefore it is difficult to fault someone for choosing either of these two options. However, because self-driving cars are programmed in advance, we must as a society decide what the best action would be for the car to take. Would the programmer who decides which action the car should take be held responsible if the car is unable to stop in time to prevent injuring the child? Or would the programmer be held responsible if the car swerves and injures the “driver” after hitting a tree? There does not seem to be an easy answer to these questions, however, if we are going to instruct self-driving cars to act in ways that produce the best outcomes, it is necessary for us to decide what this looks like and how it is manifested in various real-world examples.
	With these questions in mind, it is important that we consider the legal and ethical dilemmas resulting from advances in self-driving car capabilities, before we are ready to implement them throughout our society.

output_text:
Self driving cars may seem too futuristic to believe, but they have become a reality. They are a symbol of technological advancement we have been able to achieve. They bring with them the hope of increased road safety and awareness. When they become a reality they will improve upon the transportation transportation industry in efficiency and lower costs. Although the huge benefits, self driving cars are still far from reality to be commercialized. They come with huge costs to bear in terms of equipment. The amount of personal information stored in them might make a few people uncomfortable. As well as, self-driving cars will not be able to perform without their sensors in extreme climate conditions.

While there is a lot of skepticism involving self driving cars, I think they will improve road safety and help with accessibility around the United States.

Clearly, a primary benefit of self-driving cars lies in their potential to dramatically enhance road safety by mitigating the pervasive problem of human error. Unlike human drivers, autonomous vehicles (AVs) operate with constant vigilance, unaffected by fatigue, distraction, or impairment. Their sophisticated algorithms continuously monitor the vehicle's surroundings using an array of sensors, calculating appropriate stopping distances and reaction times with precision far exceeding human capabilities. This technology eliminates common causes of accidents such as texting while driving, falling asleep at the wheel, or even momentary lapses in attention, which are responsible for a vast majority of collisions. The consistent, data-driven decision-making of AVs promises a future with fewer accidents, injuries, and fatalities.

Beyond safety, self-driving cars offer an unprecedented opportunity to expand accessibility for millions, fundamentally transforming personal mobility. For senior citizens who may lose the ability to drive due to age or health, and for individuals with disabilities, AVs represent a lifeline to independence and freedom. They can access reliable and safe transportation without relying on others, enabling them to maintain social connections, attend appointments, and participate fully in daily life. Furthermore, this technology can bridge transportation gaps in large cities or remotely connected areas that lack robust public transport infrastructure. By allowing cars to operate around the clock, unaffected by human fatigue, self-driving fleets can provide on-demand mobility, ensuring that transportation is available whenever and wherever it is needed, thereby enhancing equity in access to essential services and opportunities.

The widespread adoption of self-driving cars also promises significant societal and environmental advantages, extending their positive impact beyond individual users. By removing human drivers, AVs are expected to drastically reduce incidents of road rage and drunk driving, creating calmer and safer roadways for everyone. This shift would free up law enforcement to focus on other critical issues, optimizing public safety resources. Moreover, as many self-driving vehicles are designed to be electric, their integration into the transportation system will accelerate the transition away from fossil fuels. This will lead to a substantial reduction in air pollution, particularly in urban centers, and contribute to global efforts against climate change, simultaneously increasing energy efficiency and promoting sustainable urban environments.

Furthermore, self-driving technology holds the potential to revolutionize urban planning and significantly alleviate traffic congestion. Imagine a future where vehicles no longer need to occupy prime real estate for parking. Self-driving cars can drop off passengers at their destinations and then autonomously proceed to designated, less central parking facilities or even continue circulating as part of a shared fleet. This "drop-off and go" model would drastically reduce the demand for parking spaces, freeing up valuable urban land for parks, housing, or businesses. Additionally, the optimized routing and smoother traffic flow enabled by interconnected AVs would minimize stop-and-go traffic, decreasing travel times and fuel consumption, thereby creating more efficient and livable cities.

Despite their advanced underlying technology, the successful integration of self-driving cars into everyday life hinges on their user-friendliness and intuitive design. Developers are keenly aware that widespread adoption requires systems that are not only safe and efficient but also easy for the average person to understand and operate. The goal is to design interfaces that are compatible with the existing driving experience, minimizing the need for extensive special training. Through clear visual cues, voice commands, and automated decision-making processes, self-driving cars will be engineered to feel familiar and accessible, allowing users to fully leverage the technology without intimidation. This focus on human-centered design will be crucial in overcoming initial public skepticism and facilitating a smooth transition to autonomous mobility.

While the current cost of constructing self-driving vehicles remains a significant hurdle, this financial barrier is projected to diminish rapidly as the technology matures and economies of scale take effect. The initial high price tag is largely due to the expensive components like LiDAR sensors and advanced computing hardware, coupled with extensive research and development. However, history shows that as computational costs decrease and manufacturing processes become more efficient, groundbreaking technologies become increasingly affordable. Government initiatives, such as tax incentives for AV adoption, infrastructure investments, and funding for R&D, will also play a crucial role in lowering costs and accelerating commercialization. These factors combined will ensure that what is currently a high-end luxury will eventually become accessible to the broader population, democratizing this incredible technology.

Another valid concern often raised about self-driving cars is the potential for privacy breaches given the vast amounts of personal and locational data they collect. However, this challenge is being addressed proactively through robust cybersecurity measures and evolving regulatory frameworks. Manufacturers are implementing advanced encryption protocols to protect sensitive data from unauthorized access, similar to the security standards applied to smartphones and other connected devices. Furthermore, governments and industry bodies are working to establish clear guidelines and legislation to govern data collection, storage, and usage in autonomous vehicles, ensuring transparency and user control. Just as with other digital technologies, the benefits of enhanced safety and convenience can be realized while simultaneously upholding stringent privacy standards through continuous innovation and vigilant oversight.

Critics also point to the current limitations of self-driving cars in extreme climate conditions, arguing that sensors may struggle with heavy rain, snow, or fog. While this remains an area of active research and development, significant progress is being made to enhance the resilience of autonomous systems. Engineers are developing multi-modal sensor fusion technologies that combine the strengths of various sensors (e.g., radar, LiDAR, cameras, ultrasonic sensors) to compensate for individual weaknesses in adverse weather. Advanced AI algorithms are also being trained on vast datasets of challenging weather scenarios, allowing AVs to interpret complex environmental cues more effectively. Future solutions may also involve vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication, where smart road infrastructure provides real-time environmental data to vehicles, further improving their performance and safety regardless of the weather, thereby gradually overcoming these environmental challenges.

Self-driving cars, despite their current challenges, undeniably represent a monumental leap forward in transportation technology. They promise a future where roads are safer, accessibility is universal, and urban environments are more efficient and sustainable. While concerns regarding cost, privacy, and environmental performance are valid, they are being systematically addressed through technological innovation, proactive regulation, and market evolution. The journey to fully autonomous vehicles is ongoing, but the trajectory is clear: self-driving cars are poised to redefine our relationship with transportation, offering a future that is not only more convenient but profoundly better for individuals and society as a whole.

output_text:
Self-driving cars will be the most innovative technology of the 21st century. I want to eagerly wait in line for these cars. Just think, you get into your car, can sightsee, have a martini, or do work at home in your car. What an exciting prospect! These smart cars will eliminate traffic jams with the platooning effect, maintaining the proper speed instead of the impatient human driver speeding when there is a jam. We won't have to pay for a useless cop pulling cars for minor reasons; think of all the taxes we save not having to pay them. Also, no more car insurance—such a refreshing thought! Instead of all these lazy humans looking for the closest parking space to shopping centers, the cars could just drop them off. Maybe they would be forced to walk a bit, getting some much-needed exercise. There are some quirks to figure out, but there are some very intelligent, educated engineers who would be able to figure out the disadvantages of self-driving cars. Also, legally if there was an accident, it would be a human at fault anyway, so that would eventually be worked out. As far as loss of jobs or displacement, there are many job openings in retail, fast food, and nursing homes; these workers could all find some sort of employment or they would maybe have to be reeducated. Just think of how cheap goods would be: instead of depending on a dumb, uneducated truck driver, the self-driving car would deliver goods, and it could go for 24 hours. Great for the environment—less trucks on the road! The educated would be able to afford the cars in the beginning; however, as with all technology, the price would come down, making them more affordable to the masses. Also, a crippled person could just hop in a self-driving car to go to all those doctors' appointments. No more of these access buses clogging up our city streets. The smart self-driving cars with their algorithms would be perfection; they would eliminate accidents, would obey all speed limits, lights, and stop signs. This would save our court system so much time and money. We wouldn't be dragged into court for these matters, and, most excitingly, we could eliminate the ambulance-chasing snake lawyers! So many advantages to self-driving cars, saving us so much time and money. By the way, the infrastructure to operate these cars would create millions of excellent jobs, and everyone would be able to afford a self-driving car. I will be waiting in line when they are ready to drive.

Beyond the immediate convenience of in-car leisure and productivity, autonomous vehicles promise a radical transformation of urban mobility by virtually eliminating traffic congestion and optimizing travel times. Research by institutions like the Eno Center for Transportation suggests that widespread adoption of self-driving cars, particularly with platooning capabilities, could reduce highway capacity needs by 80% and decrease commute times by over 20%. This efficiency stems from the cars' ability to maintain optimal speeds, communicate with each other, and dynamically adjust routes, preventing the bottlenecks caused by human error and impatient driving. Such advancements would not only save countless hours currently lost to gridlock but also significantly reduce fuel consumption and emissions, contributing to a healthier environment by minimizing idle time and optimizing traffic flow.

The economic and societal benefits extend far beyond traffic management. The elimination of human error, which accounts for over 90% of all road accidents, would drastically reduce fatalities and injuries, easing the burden on emergency services and healthcare systems. Consider the financial implications: insurance premiums, currently a significant expense for drivers, could see substantial reductions or even fundamental restructuring as fault becomes clearer and risks are minimized. Furthermore, the efficiency of autonomous commercial vehicles, operating 24/7 without mandatory rest breaks, could dramatically lower shipping costs, leading to cheaper goods for consumers. This revolutionary shift would also free up public funds currently allocated to traffic enforcement and court proceedings for minor driving infractions, redirecting resources to more pressing community needs.

Moreover, self-driving cars offer unprecedented accessibility and new economic opportunities. For individuals with disabilities, the elderly, or those unable to drive, autonomous vehicles provide a newfound freedom of movement, allowing them to attend appointments, visit friends, and participate more fully in society without relying on public transport or specialized services. This independence not only improves quality of life but also reduces the demand for costly access buses, streamlining urban transit. On the economic front, the massive infrastructure development required to support these smart cars, from advanced sensors to communication networks, will generate millions of high-skilled jobs in engineering, software development, installation, and maintenance, offsetting potential job losses in traditional driving sectors.

While the transformative potential of self-driving cars is undeniable, critics often raise concerns regarding job displacement, initial costs, and legal ambiguities. It is true that professions like truck driving or taxi services may see significant changes, but history shows that technological advancements consistently create new industries and job roles. As mentioned, the infrastructure and maintenance of autonomous fleets will require a new workforce, and retraining programs can help transition displaced workers into fields such as data analytics, cybersecurity, and specialized vehicle repair. Regarding cost, like all groundbreaking technologies, self-driving cars will initially be expensive, but economies of scale and competitive markets will inevitably drive prices down, making them accessible to the masses, much like personal computers or smartphones. Finally, legal frameworks for accident liability are actively being developed, with many jurisdictions leaning towards manufacturer responsibility in cases of system failure, simplifying the process and potentially reducing protracted litigation. These challenges are not insurmountable but rather opportunities for innovation and adaptation.

In conclusion, self-driving cars stand poised to be the most innovative technology of the 21st century, promising a future of unprecedented efficiency, safety, and economic prosperity. From revolutionizing daily commutes and enhancing personal freedom to creating new jobs and fundamentally improving our legal and environmental landscapes, the advantages are profound and far-reaching. While challenges such as job displacement and initial costs require careful planning and adaptation, these are minor hurdles compared to the immense benefits. The era of autonomous vehicles is not merely an upgrade; it is a paradigm shift that will redefine our relationship with transportation, saving us invaluable time, money, and, most importantly, lives. The future of driving is intelligent, efficient, and inevitable, and I, for one, will be eagerly waiting in line.

draft1:
In today's age we are always looking for the 'next thing' in terms of technological advancement. Google is one company that continuously strives to answer that call. Recently they have been doing so by researching the technology for and creating self-driving cars. While this may sound like some futuristic fabrication from Back to the Future, the technology is actually here and being tested even as this piece is being written. 
  Self-driving cars would drastically change our mobility. Human error accounts for about 81 percent of car accidents; if everyone were to transition to self-drivings cars, human error would be eliminated and accidents would be projected to drastically decrease. Traffic patterns themselves would change. The technology in the car would be able to detect its proximity to other cars and adjust its speed and positioning accordingly. This has the potential to make roadways more efficient and commute shorter. One of the most attractive aspects of a self-driving car is the convenience that they provide. The New York Times has suggested that these cars would be a great asset to individuals with disabilities; instead of relying on public transportation they would be able to operate their car as anyone else would. These cars would also be an asset to an individual who wants to get work done in the time it takes to make their commute; they could safely work on a laptop or hold a conversation without having to worry about driving. 
  At this point you're thinking, 'this seems great, sign me up!', right? Well, you might want to re-consider. Self-driving cars sound great in theory but thinking through their practical applications one runs into several road blocks (pun intended). Consider this. A person gets into their car while intoxicated. In theory, this wouldn't be a problem because the car can simply drive itself and the individual can safely ride in the back without endangering themself or others. The car comes upon a traffic signal that is not operating correctly and there is a traffic cop directly the flow of cars. The self-driving car is not equipped to deal with human signals; it does not have human judgement. Instead of being able to over-ride the autonomic function of the car the individual is incapacitated themself and so the car speeds through the signal resulting in an accident where several people are injured. Whose fault is this? The intoxicated individual? The car? The other cars involved? The answer is not clear cut here but the result is the same as if an intoxicated individual had chosen to manually drive the car.
  Humans are not perfect. But then again, neither is technology. Both may have the ability to drive a car but is one really better than the other? My response would be no. A self-driving car is an amazing feat of technology. It is a tool that can be used for many positive purposes and has the potential to change the way in which humans travel. But self-driving cars should only be that: a tool. Humans should still retain the capacity to manually drive a car should the technology glitch and fail for both their safety and the safety of others.

	Self-driving cars would drastically change our mobility. Human error accounts for about 81 percent of car accidents; if everyone were to transition to self-drivings cars, human error would, theoretically, be eliminated and accidents would be projected to drastically decrease. Traffic patterns themselves would change. The technology in the car would be able to detect its proximity to other cars and adjust its speed and positioning accordingly, making roadways more efficient. Speed limits could increase since the technology in the cars would have the capacity to calculate a safe velocity and adjust the speed accordingly leading to reduced commute times. 
         One of the most attractive aspects of a self-driving car is the convenience that it provides. The New York Times has suggested that these cars would be a great asset to individuals with disabilities; instead of relying on public transportation they would be able to operate their car as anyone else would. These cars would also be an asset to an individual who wants to get work done in the time it takes to make their commute; they could safely work on a laptop or hold a conversation without having to worry about driving. Running late for a meeting and worried about parking? Not a problem! Self-driving cars would also be able to self-park allowing you to pull up right to the curb and not have to frantically find a parking spot. 
	At this point you're thinking, 'this seems great, sign me up!', right? Well, you might want to re-consider. Self-driving cars sound great in theory but when thinking through their practical applications one runs into several road blocks (pun intended). Consider this. A person gets into their car while intoxicated. This shouldn't be a problem because the car can simply drive itself; the individual can safely ride in the car without endangering themself or others through reckless driving. The car comes upon a traffic signal that is not operating correctly and there is a traffic cop directly the flow of cars. The self-driving car is not equipped to deal with human signals: it does not have human judgement. Instead of being able to over-ride the autonomic function of the car the individual is incapacitated and so the car speeds through the signal resulting in an accident where several people are injured. Who is at fault? The intoxicated individual? The car? The other cars involved? The answer is not clear here but the result is the same as if an intoxicated individual had chosen to get behind the wheel and manually drive the car. 
	Humans are not perfect. But then again, neither is technology. Both may have the ability to drive a car but is one really better than the other? My response would be no. A self-driving car is an amazing feat of technology. It is a tool that can be used for many positive purposes and has the potential to change the way in which humans travel. But self-driving cars should only be that: a tool. The technology that drives these cars has the ability to fail such as in the scenario I described above as well as through security breaches or simple technology glitches. All scenarios must be thoroughly researched and discussed prior to the mass-production of this technology. The technology has to be made simply enough so that, in the event of a glitch, a person can manually operate the vehicle. At the same time, it needs to be complex enough so that hacker's cannot readily hijack the technology and take control of the car. 
        Does this mean that research on and manufacturing of self-driving cars should be halted or terminated? Of course not! New technology gives us the ability to improve our quality of life. If self-driving cars were to be utilized in mass, there would be a myriad of benefits that should not be overlooked. Prior to the adoption of mass-production, we must be careful to define the role that human's will play while inside the vehicles. Humans should still retain the capacity to manually drive a car should the technology glitch and fail both for the safety of the driver and the safety of others. Ultimately, if you choose to get into a car whether or not you are the one driving, you should have the ability to take control of the vehicle - or at least maintain an awareness of your surroundings in the event you have to make spur of the moment decisions while on the road. 
	Thankfully, we have time to carefully think through all sides of the self-driving car conversation before they are able to be mass produced. Currently, the price of the cars are astronomically high: over $100,000! With only the one percent of society being able to afford them we don't have to worry too much about how these cars will change our current transportation system. However, the sooner these topics are discussed the easier it will be to resolve potential problems that arise regarding these cars in the future. Self-driving cars are a testament to how far technology has come. Learning how to safely use this new technology will be the cornerstone upon which the technology ultimately proves to help or hinder our mobility and the transportation industry as a whole.

draft1:
As with any new invention, technological or otherwise, there is both major benefits that make the research and development worthwhile and equally major concerns that come from unknown consequences of the new product. For self-driving cars, the benefits are obvious: reducing opportunities for human error on the road, increasing accessibility to those who would be unable to drive manually, improving traffic flow, and saving time. However, all of those benefits come at a high financial cost with several unknown variables such as data security, influence of those drivers not in self-driving cars, potential failures of car or road technology, and legal conundrums. With major companies such as Google investing substantial sums of money into the research and development of self-driving cars, it's apparent that enough people see the benefits eventually outweighing the cost. However, without more conversation about the education that drivers would need to have to successfully operate self-driving cars, driver-less cars will not be as beneficial as some might think.

Though an article in Auto Insurance Center, "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated with Self-Driving Cars," lists not needing to go to the DMV to get a specialized driving license as an advantage of self-driving cars, that is clearly not the case. 

As discussed in the "cons" section, other sources including Teletrac note that drivers would still have to be trained in how to properly operate the vehicle. Further, if a potential benefit is, as mentioned in the article, that people who can't normally operate cars, such as those who are disable, elderly, or intoxicated, the fact remains that even if trained on how to properly operate a self-driving car, these individuals may not have the physical or mental capacity to manually drive the vehicle.

The article also makes the point that over-relying on the technology could mean that drivers would no longer have the skills to over-ride the system and manually control the vehicle in the event of a failure. Thus, self-driving cars actually require more education: both standard self-driving car operating procedures as well as regular driving training.

With the proper education, one can see the potential benefits mentioned in the article: reduction of distracted driving, more time for other activities while in the car, and travel efficiency.

Increased education may be a mediator of the potential advantages and dangers, but ultimately self-driving cars is a misleading name, as an operator would always have to step in should a system fail within the car or an occurrence of an unusual road condition. Therefore, all stated benefits have their limits, since drivers will need to be paying enough attention to take over in an instant in an emergency situation.

However, it also mentions that hacking is a safety concern for many reporting sources, including the Guardian. output_text:
As with any new invention, technological or otherwise, there is both major benefits that make the research and development worthwhile and equally major concerns that come from unknown consequences of the new product. For self-driving cars, the benefits are obvious: reducing opportunities for human error on the road, increasing accessibility to those who would be unable to drive manually, improving traffic flow, and saving time. However, all of those benefits come at a high financial cost with several unknown variables such as data security, influence of those drivers not in self-driving cars, potential failures of car or road technology, and legal conundrums. With major companies such as Google investing substantial sums of money into the research and development of self-driving cars, it's apparent that enough people see the benefits eventually outweighing the cost. However, without more conversation about the education that drivers would need to have to successfully operate self-driving cars, driver-less cars will not be as beneficial as some might think.

Though an article in Auto Insurance Center, "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated with Self-Driving Cars," lists not needing to go to the DMV to get a specialized driving license as an advantage of self-driving cars, that is clearly not the case. As discussed in the "cons" section, other sources including Teletrac note that drivers would still have to be trained in how to properly operate the vehicle. This training is not merely a formality but a critical necessity, encompassing not only the car’s autonomous functions but also the protocols for intervention and manual override.

Further, if a potential benefit is, as mentioned in the article, that people who can't normally operate cars, such as those who are disabled, elderly, or intoxicated, could gain mobility, the fact remains that even if trained on how to properly operate a self-driving car, these individuals may not have the physical or mental capacity to manually drive the vehicle in an emergency. The promise of increased accessibility for these groups is thus significantly undermined by the persistent requirement for human oversight and the potential need for immediate manual intervention. The physical and cognitive demands of safely taking over a malfunctioning autonomous system, or navigating an unexpected road hazard, cannot be dismissed, rendering the "driver-less" label a misnomer for many who currently lack driving capabilities.

The article also makes the point that over-relying on the technology could mean that drivers would no longer have the skills to over-ride the system and manually control the vehicle in the event of a failure. Thus, self-driving cars actually require more education: both standard self-driving car operating procedures as well as regular driving training to maintain manual proficiency. This dual educational requirement introduces a paradox: while the technology aims to reduce human error, it simultaneously demands a higher, more vigilant form of human engagement, lest critical manual skills erode over time. The expectation that a driver, accustomed to automation, can instantaneously transition to manual control during a crisis places an immense, and perhaps unrealistic, burden on human adaptability.

This constant need for human readiness also introduces complex legal and ethical dilemmas, which were among the "unknown variables" initially cited. In the event of an accident, determining liability becomes convoluted. If a self-driving car malfunctions, but the human operator fails to intervene due to a lapse in attention or skill, who bears responsibility? This ambiguity could lead to protracted legal battles and a lack of clear accountability, potentially eroding public trust in the technology. Therefore, alongside technical education, a robust framework for legal responsibility and driver expectations must be established to ensure both safety and fairness.

With the proper education, one can see the potential benefits mentioned in the article: reduction of distracted driving, more time for other activities while in the car, and travel efficiency. However, even these advantages are tempered by the imperative for ongoing driver awareness. Increased education may be a mediator of the potential advantages and dangers, but ultimately self-driving cars is a misleading name, as an operator would always have to step in should a system fail within the car or an occurrence of an unusual road condition. Therefore, all stated benefits have their limits, since drivers will need to be paying enough attention to take over in an instant in an emergency situation, effectively transforming the "driver-less" experience into one of constant, albeit passive, vigilance.

In conclusion, while self-driving cars offer tantalizing benefits such as enhanced safety and increased accessibility, these advantages are contingent upon a fundamental re-evaluation of the driver's role. The notion that these vehicles eliminate the need for driver education, or even a driver, is a dangerous oversimplification. As evidence from sources like Auto Insurance Center and Teletrac suggests, extensive training in both autonomous operation and manual override is essential. Furthermore, the persistent need for human intervention limits the benefits for those currently unable to drive and introduces complex questions of liability. Without a comprehensive and ongoing commitment to driver education, coupled with a clear understanding of human responsibility, the transformative potential of self-driving cars will remain largely unfulfilled, and their perceived benefits will be overshadowed by the unaddressed complexities of human-machine interaction.

output_text:
Many car accidents are preventable, and an alarming number of them are a result of distracted driving. Self-driving cars might not be the answer.

Equipped with multiple sensors and advanced algorithms, a self-driving car is more likely to be an ideal driver; a self-driving car is more accurate, reliable, and focused than a human driver. However, engineers are still researching self-driving technology, and many practical questions need to be answered. For example, who will drive the car if the technology fails? This op-ed article will convince you that we should not use self-driving cars.

First, like humans, technology could fail. Who will shoulder the responsibility of driving when the technology fails? We know that there is a big circulating head on top of a self-driving car. It is the “eye” and “ear” of the car. When a human driver is navigating through a storm, they use their senses to make informed decisions. However, these sophisticated sensors, while robust, are vulnerable to extreme weather conditions like heavy snow, torrential rain, or thick fog, which can obscure their vision or even physically damage them. A “blind” driver, whether human or algorithmic, cannot safely operate a car, and the car's inability to perceive its surroundings accurately could lead to catastrophic failures. In addition, if reliance on autonomous systems becomes widespread, it could discourage prospective drivers from attending comprehensive driver's education courses, reducing the general public's proficiency in manual driving. In the critical event of technology failure, these unskilled human operators would be ill-prepared to quickly and safely assume control, potentially escalating a minor malfunction into a severe accident. The consequences could range from property damage to serious injuries or fatalities, all due to a lack of essential human driving skills.

Who should be responsible for the consequences caused by a malfunctioning self-driving car? When a self-driving car malfunctions, the people inside of the car may not be able to gain control quickly. The time lapsed between a mechanical failure and an attempt to gain control over the car might be long enough to cause an accident. Even a momentary delay as a human attempts to override an automated system and regain control could be the difference between avoiding an obstacle and a devastating collision, especially at highway speeds or in complex urban environments. In a court of law, establishing legal responsibility becomes a labyrinthine challenge. Is the car's owner, who was merely a passenger, liable? Or is it the car manufacturer, the software developer, or the sensor provider? The current legal framework is ill-equipped to handle such nuanced cases, potentially leading to prolonged disputes and inadequate compensation for victims. This ambiguity undermines public trust and creates an unacceptable legal vacuum regarding accountability.

Proponents of self-driving cars argue that self-driving cars reduce the cost of driving and alleviate societal burden. On the contrary, the cost of self-driving cars is much higher than expected. The extensive research and development costs that companies now pour into testing, along with the continuous need for software updates and infrastructure investments, will inevitably be passed on to consumers through higher purchase prices, subscription services, or increased public taxes. In addition, self-driving cars are equipped with sensors that carry a high maintenance and replacement fee. A self-driving car is still a luxury for the general public, and if only a select few can afford these advanced vehicles, the promised societal benefits, such as reduced traffic congestion or fewer accidents, will remain largely unrealized, failing to address the broader issues of modern transportation. Additionally, the widespread adoption of self-driving technology would lead to significant job displacement within the transportation industry, as autonomous vehicles replace human drivers in roles such as taxi services, ride-sharing, and long-haul trucking. This economic disruption would impact millions of livelihoods, creating a severe societal burden rather than alleviating it. Furthermore, given that many self-driving vehicles are designed to be electric, a large-scale transition would severely impact the gasoline industry, leading to further job losses and economic instability in sectors reliant on fossil fuels. These profound economic and social shifts represent significant drawbacks that far outweigh the speculative benefits.

In conclusion, while the vision of a future free from distracted driving is appealing, the current reality of self-driving technology presents too many unresolved challenges and potential pitfalls. The risks associated with technological failures, the complexities of legal liability, the potential for human unpreparedness in emergencies, and the substantial economic and social costs far outweigh the touted advantages. Instead of rushing to embrace a technology that is not yet fully mature or universally beneficial, our efforts should focus on improving human driving education, enhancing existing safety features, and addressing the root causes of distracted driving. Until these critical issues are adequately resolved, a cautious approach that prioritizes human oversight and responsibility remains the safest and most responsible path forward for our roads and our society.

Self-driving cars have beneficial and disadvantageous effects on society. Although the benefits of self-driving cars may seem promising, the adverse effects might deter one from investing in them as the future of automobile technology. First, let’s review the positive side of self-driving cars. 

Self-driving cars would allow parents to feel safer about their high-school children “driving” to school with a reduced risk of speeding or an accident. If their child is distracted by a text, it wouldn’t matter because he/she wouldn’t need to pay attention to the road. If everyone had self-driving cars, there would be much fewer accidents, and speeding would be a thing of the past, allowing police officers more time and manpower to devote to other crimes. These self-driving cars would also benefit the sick and the elderly who would otherwise need to depend on others to get around. 

From an industrial growth prospective, the move toward self-driving cars would promote job growth for minds with a knack for technological advancement and machine learning. A new industry would be created. This can translate to the public transportation sector; self-driving buses and trains may soon follow. 

However, although there are many positives of self-driving cars, as with any technological advancements, there are also risks. Those with self-driving cars may need to worry about keeping their data secure from hackers who may want to know a person’s most frequently visited locations. This data could be used in targeted advertisements, or for more malicious purposes such as ascertaining the best place for a potential robbery or when the home would be empty. In adverse weather or road conditions that would provide the self-driving car with less-clear visual data, there could be a greater risk for an accident. If the occupants of the car do not pay careful attention, they may end up in a car accident. Even more alarming, if a self-driving car is on a slippery road and spins out of control, does it jerk out of the way to save a child pedestrian or the fit male pedestrian? The car wouldn’t know how to make these “gut-feeling” decisions. Additionally, if an accident does happen, where does the blame lie? A car cannot be charged with vehicular manslaughter. If the driver was not technically in control of the vehicle, they may not be responsible either. All that is left is the manufacturing company, however that doesn’t seem appropriate either. New legislation would need to be implemented to account for these possible scenarios.

Whereas some industries may see job growth, public transportation personnel would have a job reduction, as would taxi drivers. The oil and gasoline industries may also suffer, potentially harming export sales from countries that would need it. If these self-driving cars are mostly electric, many gas stations may go out of business. 

Thus, although self-driving cars may provide some benefits to society, the overall risks and potential harm is far greater. People would lose the ability to do things for themselves, others may exploit holes in the technology’s security to harm others, and industries may collapse due to the sudden shift in technology that would ultimately change how the world functions.

As technology continues to progress at a rapid pace, innovators have begun to explore the possibility of self-driving cars. A self-driving car sounds like a child’s fantasy of what a futuristic dystopian society looks like. However, self-driving cars are no longer a fantasy idea, and the impact that these vehicles may have on society is immense. Self-driving cars bring about thoughts of pure leisure while driving. With a self-driving car, there is no more need to be stressed out while driving because something else is taking care of all the driving. A self-driving car could help to limit accidents due to the fact that we are taking away the human error aspect of driving. These cars will be programmed to simply avoid accidents and obey all traffic laws. This, however, is a very idealistic way of thinking. Self-driving cars still bring about their fair share of negative impacts on society. While it may seem that self-driving cars are taking out the human error aspect of driving, they are simply replacing it with another opportunity for human error. There is still a strong possibility of human error in the programming of the vehicles. The algorithms could be miscalculated or the technology of the vehicle could be faulty, resulting in a similar dangerousness to an average human driver. That is why it would not be beneficial to society to continue to push for the implementation of self-driving cars.
	The risks of self-driving cars are too great to overcome when it comes to implementing them into society. One argument for self-driving cars is that they will not be distracted drivers since they will have an algorithm to follow while on the road. While it is true that human drivers face hundreds of distractions while driving, they still have some human reactions and instincts that a machine cannot replicate. The main concern is that the decision-making aspects of a self-driving car may in theory sound better than the decision-making of an average human; however, a machine cannot make up for the judgment of a human being. A scenario in which a self-driving car is driving steadily down the road past a grocery store when suddenly a shopping cart comes directly into its path and it does not have enough time to stop. Algorithms would say that the car would most likely swerve to avoid the shopping cart. But say there is a human being crossing the street right next to the shopping cart. How will the self-driving car know that it would be better to brace the impact of the cart directly while avoiding the human? It is not clear that a self-driving car would have the judgment and instincts to process that decision in a matter of milliseconds. On the other hand, a human would at least be capable of making the conscious decision to avoid the human.
    Beyond the immediate safety concerns, the widespread implementation of self-driving cars poses a significant threat to the livelihoods of millions. The advent of autonomous vehicles would inevitably lead to massive job displacement across the transportation sector. Professional drivers, including taxi and ride-share operators, long-haul truckers, and delivery personnel, constitute a substantial portion of the global workforce. Their roles, which rely on human navigation and interaction, would become largely redundant. While proponents might argue that new jobs would emerge in areas like vehicle maintenance and software development, the scale of displacement would far exceed the creation of new opportunities, leading to widespread unemployment and severe economic disruption in communities heavily reliant on these industries. This societal upheaval represents a tangible negative impact that cannot be easily mitigated.
    Furthermore, the reliance on complex software and network connectivity introduces a new frontier of risk: cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Unlike human drivers, whose errors are typically isolated incidents, a self-driving car system could be compromised on a mass scale by malicious actors. Hacking attempts could range from data breaches exposing personal travel patterns to far more catastrophic scenarios, such as ransomware attacks disabling entire fleets or even remote manipulation of vehicles to cause accidents or traffic chaos. The potential for cyber-terrorism, where autonomous vehicles could be weaponized, presents an alarming prospect. Ensuring the inviolability of such intricate systems would be an ongoing, monumental challenge, and any failure could have widespread, devastating consequences, far beyond what any individual human error could achieve.
While this is simply one scenario of thousands, it is clear that self-driving cars lack the human instinct and decision-making that no form of algorithm can replicate. Coupled with the substantial threat of widespread job displacement and the unprecedented cybersecurity vulnerabilities inherent in autonomous systems, the risks associated with self-driving cars are too great to overlook. The potential for programming errors, the absence of human judgment in critical ethical dilemmas, the destabilizing economic impact, and the looming specter of cyber-attacks collectively underscore a future fraught with peril. It is prudent and safest to avoid implementing the idea of self-driving cars, as their negative outcomes do not seem to outweigh the benefits they would have on society. Until these multifaceted and profound challenges can be definitively addressed, society should prioritize caution over rapid technological advancement in this domain.

draft1:
As with the advent and introduction of any new technology, there are pros and cons that arise with the implementation of that technology. This is also true with autonomous, or self-driving, cars. With the testing of self-driving cars currently happening in several cities across the United States, the debate on whether or not autonomous cars are safer than those driven by humans has come to the forefront of the public health and wellness debate in the media. Many cars already come equipped with some of the basic technology - autonomous breaking, self-parking, or sensors that detect close objects, so we may be closer to the future than we think.

There are several pros for the use of self-driving cars, and one of the biggest is safety. While a small percentage of car accidents could be caused by mechanical failures, the majority are caused by a distracted driver. A computer driver would take the human element factor out of the equation and remove most of the danger just by being a computer. Computers make for the ideal driver as they will not get tired, lose their eyesight, get distracted, imbibe in drugs or alcohol, etc. Computers also use specific algorithms to calculate speed, stopping distance, and other collected information that help to dramatically decrease the chance of an accident. 

In addition to safety, there are many other positive side-effects to autonomous driving. Self-driving cars could help with traffic optimization, as the cars could communicate with each other to help identify traffic problems and their specific algorithms and sensors could help to reduce commute times while also allowing more cars on the road with less traffic. People with disabilities would have more options at their disposal. Police could focus less time on traffic stops and more time on other crimes. People would have more time to do other thigns with less parking and DMV headaches for drivers. The benefits are endless. 

With all the good things that come with technology, there are bound to be some bad things, as well. One of the biggest downfalls anticipated with self-driving cars would be their affect on the economy. Not only is the cost of implementing the new technology more expensive than most Americans can afford, it could also eliminate a lot of jobs in transportation. It is also expected that since most self-driving cars are powered by computers, that they would be electric cars, further putting a strain on the gas industry. Reliance on technology could make a driver's license, insurance, the DMV, and other driving related vendors obsolete. The benefits of self-driving cars would also depend upon more people opting into the option. 

Other downfalls to autonomous cars would be the flaws with technology itself. A computer cannot detect a road block or read human hand signals, so road construction would be an obstacle. The sensors are located on the outside of the vehicle, so severe weather can damage them. Computers attract hackers and software requires user information, so there is always a chance for breach of security. Another risk would be the technology failure where a person must take over the controls - what if it is someone that has never driven their own car before? What if the GPS goes down? How will a person be able to get to where they need to go?

While a completely autonomous driving society is probably a ways off, society certainly seems to be moving towards it. I do believe that self-driving cars would be a benefit in many ways - safety, inclusion, environment, and time/traffic - but I think we have some questions that need to be answered before we get there. While there may be jobs that are lost in transportation, there will be jobs that are created with the technology and manufacturing of these vehicles. The inclusion of people with disabilities, the elderly, and those that do not have a license is a powerful tool for those that were previously reliant on others. In my opinion, self-driving cars will get over the hump and be a part of the near future, at least in a small realm.

As with the advent and introduction of any new technology, there are pros and cons that arise with its implementation. This holds true for autonomous, or self-driving, cars. With testing currently underway in several cities across the United States, the debate on whether or not autonomous cars are safer than those driven by humans has come to the forefront of public discourse. While the transition to a fully autonomous fleet presents legitimate challenges regarding economic disruption and technological limitations, the profound potential for enhanced safety, increased accessibility, and optimized traffic flow ultimately positions self-driving vehicles as a crucial and beneficial evolution for society.

One of the most compelling arguments for the widespread adoption of self-driving cars is the significant improvement in road safety. While a small percentage of vehicular accidents can be attributed to mechanical failures, the overwhelming majority stem from human error, including distracted driving, fatigue, impairment, or aggressive behavior. Autonomous vehicles eliminate this volatile human element from the driving equation. Programmed to operate without distraction, drowsiness, or influence from substances, computer drivers adhere strictly to traffic laws and optimal driving parameters. Furthermore, these systems utilize sophisticated algorithms to constantly calculate speed, stopping distances, and environmental data, dramatically decreasing the likelihood of collisions by reacting faster and more consistently than human drivers ever could.

Beyond safety, the integration of autonomous vehicles promises a host of other societal benefits. Traffic optimization stands as a key advantage, as self-driving cars could communicate with each other and infrastructure to identify and alleviate congestion, potentially reducing commute times and allowing for more efficient use of road space. For individuals with disabilities or the elderly who are unable to drive, autonomous cars offer unprecedented independence and mobility, greatly enhancing their quality of life. Furthermore, a reduction in traffic violations could free up law enforcement to focus on more serious crimes, while the minimized need for parking and simplified licensing processes could significantly reduce everyday logistical headaches for commuters.

However, the path to a fully autonomous future is not without its considerable drawbacks, particularly concerning economic implications. The initial cost of implementing this advanced technology could be prohibitive for many Americans, creating a divide in access. More significantly, the widespread adoption of self-driving vehicles threatens to displace millions of jobs in the transportation sector, including truck drivers, taxi and ride-share operators, and delivery personnel. The anticipated shift towards electric autonomous vehicles would also place a severe strain on the traditional gas and oil industries. Moreover, a society reliant on autonomous driving could render entire industries, such as driver’s licensing bureaus, traditional auto insurance models, and various driving-related services, obsolete, necessitating a massive economic restructuring.

Another critical area of concern revolves around the inherent flaws and vulnerabilities of technology itself. Autonomous systems, while advanced, currently struggle with unpredictable real-world scenarios, such as interpreting complex human hand signals at construction sites or navigating roads where temporary obstacles are present. External sensors, crucial for navigation, can be easily compromised or damaged by severe weather conditions like heavy snow or rain. Furthermore, any computer-based system is susceptible to hacking, raising serious privacy and security concerns if malicious actors were to gain control of vehicles or access user data. The question of how a human driver would safely take over control in the event of technology failure, especially for individuals with limited or no prior driving experience, also presents a significant safety dilemma, as does the potential for GPS outages rendering vehicles directionless.

While a completely autonomous driving society remains a future aspiration rather than an immediate reality, society is undoubtedly moving in that direction. The potential benefits of self-driving cars, particularly in terms of safety, inclusion, environmental impact, and traffic efficiency, are compelling and represent a significant leap forward. Though concerns about job displacement are valid, the transition will also undoubtedly create new jobs in technology development, manufacturing, and maintenance, necessitating a focus on workforce retraining. The promise of greater independence for people with disabilities, the elderly, and those without licenses is a powerful argument for their adoption. In my opinion, as the technology matures and regulatory frameworks adapt, self-driving cars will overcome current hurdles and become an integral, beneficial part of our near future, at least within defined operational domains.
